title.zh: 卷六十一 左周黃列傳
title.en: Volume 61: Biographies of Zuo, Zhou, Huang
title.raw: 卷六十一 左周黃列傳 Volume 61: Biographies of Zuo, Zhou, Huang

sentences.zh: 左雄字伯豪，南*

translations.literal: Zuo Xiong, courtesy name Bohao, was a native of Nanyang
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong, whose courtesy name was Bohao, came from Nanyang commandery

translations.literal: (commandery)
translations.idiomatic: (the commandery name)

sentences.zh: **[陽]*涅陽人也。

translations.literal: **[Yang]*—of Nieyang.
translations.idiomatic: specifically of Nieyang.

sentences.zh: 安帝時，舉孝廉，稍遷冀州刺史。

translations.literal: Under Emperor An, he was recommended as filial and incorrupt, and was gradually promoted to Governor of Ji Province.
translations.idiomatic: During the reign of Emperor An, he was nominated as filial and incorrupt and rose step by step to the governorship of Ji Province.

sentences.zh: 州部多豪族，好請托，雄常閉門不與交通。

translations.literal: The province abounded in powerful clans who loved to seek favors through connections; Xiong often shut his doors and had no dealings with them.
translations.idiomatic: The region was full of powerful families who habitually pulled strings; Zuo Xiong routinely kept his gate closed and refused such traffic.

sentences.zh: 奏案貪猾二千石，無所回忌。

translations.literal: He memorialized to investigate corrupt and slippery two-thousand-dan officials, without any turning back or scruple.
translations.idiomatic: He impeached and prosecuted venal and evasive prefects and commandery governors, showing neither hesitation nor favor.

sentences.zh: 永建初，公車征拜議郎。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yongjian, he was summoned by the public carriage and appointed Gentleman Consultant.
translations.idiomatic: In the early Yongjian era he was summoned by imperial carriage and appointed a Gentleman Consultant.

sentences.zh: 時順帝新立，大臣懈怠，朝多闕政，雄數言事，其辭深切。

translations.literal: At the time Emperor Shun had newly ascended; great ministers were slack; the court had many defects in governance; Xiong repeatedly spoke on affairs, and his language was cutting and deep.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun had only just taken the throne; senior officials were lax, and policy at court was riddled with gaps. Zuo Xiong spoke out repeatedly, in language that was blunt and searching.

sentences.zh: 尚書僕射虞詡以雄有忠公節，上疏薦之曰：「臣見方今公卿以下，類多拱默，以樹恩為賢，盡節為愚，至相戒曰：『白璧不可為，容容多後福。

translations.literal: Vice Director of the Masters of Writing Yu Xu, considering that Xiong had loyal and public-minded integrity, submitted a memorial recommending him, saying: "Your subject sees that from the dukes and ministers downward, most are folded in silence, taking the cultivation of private favor as worthy and full devotion to duty as foolish, to the point of warning one another: 'The flawless white jade cannot be pursued; easy compliance brings later blessings.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Xu, Vice Director of the Masters of Writing, believing Zuo Xiong possessed the integrity of a loyal servant, presented a memorial recommending him: "I observe that today, from the highest ministers down, most stand with folded arms and say nothing, treating the currying of favor as wisdom and whole-hearted duty as folly—until they warn each other: 'Do not try to be the spotless white jade; going along with everyone wins you blessings down the road.'

sentences.zh: 』[一]伏見議郎左雄，數上封事，至引陛下身遭難□，以為警戒，實有王臣蹇蹇之節，周公謨成王之風。

translations.literal: '[1] Your subject has seen Gentleman Consultant Zuo Xiong repeatedly submit sealed memorials, even citing Your Majesty's personally suffering hardship [lacuna], taking it as a warning; he truly has the integrity of a royal minister who stands firm in adversity, and the manner of the Duke of Zhou advising King Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: [1] I have seen how Counselor Zuo Xiong has again and again submitted sealed advice, even invoking the hardships Your Majesty endured as a cautionary mirror—this is the steadfast loyalty of a true minister of the throne, in the spirit of the Duke of Zhou guiding the young King Cheng.

sentences.zh: [二]宜擢在喉舌之官，必有匡弼之益。

translations.literal: [2] It is fitting to promote him to an office of the throat and tongue; there will surely be benefit in correcting and assisting.
translations.idiomatic: [2] He should be raised to a voice at the center of government; he would surely strengthen counsel and support for the throne.

sentences.zh: 」由是拜雄尚書，再遷尚書令。

translations.literal: " Thereupon Xiong was appointed Master of Writing, and was twice promoted to Director of the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: On that basis Zuo Xiong was appointed a Master of Writing and soon advanced twice to Director of the Masters of Writing.

sentences.zh: 上疏陳事曰： 注[一]容容猶和同也。

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial stating affairs, saying: Note [1] Rongrong means harmonizing and agreeing.
translations.idiomatic: He presented a memorial on state affairs, with this gloss: Note [1] "Rongrong" means going along and agreeing with everyone.

sentences.zh: 言不可獨為白玉之清絜，當與觿人和同。

translations.literal: It means one cannot alone be the purity of white jade; one should harmonize and agree with the multitude.
translations.idiomatic: That is: you cannot insist on being alone as spotless as white jade; you must accommodate the common run of opinion.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謨，謀也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Mo means planning.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2] "Mo" means to plan or counsel.

sentences.zh: 即尚書立政、無逸篇之類也。

translations.literal: That is, such as the "Establishing the Government" and "No Indulgence" chapters of the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: He refers to pieces like "Establishing the Government" and "No Indulgence" in the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 臣聞柔遠和邇，莫大寧人，寧人之務，莫重用賢，用賢之道，必存考黜。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that to soften what is distant and harmonize what is near, nothing is greater than bringing peace to the people; the task of bringing peace to the people, nothing is more important than employing the worthy; the way of employing the worthy must lie in examination and dismissal.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that to win over the far and harmonize the near, nothing surpasses giving the people rest; to give the people rest, nothing surpasses using the worthy; and the way to use the worthy must include assessment and removal.

sentences.zh: 是以□陶對禹，貴在知人。

translations.literal: Therefore [Gao] Yao replied to Yu, valuing the knowing of men.
translations.idiomatic: Hence Gao Yao answered Yu that the crucial thing is to know your men.

sentences.zh: 「安人則惠，黎民懷之。

translations.literal: "When one brings peace to the people, they cherish him; the black-haired people cling to him."
translations.idiomatic: "When you secure the people in peace, they love you; the common folk will cleave to you."

sentences.zh: 」[一]分伯建侯，代位親民，民用和穆，禮讓以興。

translations.literal: "[1] Dividing the shepherds and establishing the lords, they succeeded one another in caring for the people; the people used harmony and reverence, and yielding and deference arose.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The realm was parcelled among regional lords who succeeded one another in caring for the people, so the people lived in concord and courtesy flourished.

sentences.zh: 故詩云：「有渰淒淒，興雨祁祁。

translations.literal: Therefore the Odes says: "There is yin-yin, chill-chill; it raises rain, slow-slow.
translations.idiomatic: So the Classic of Poetry says: "Dark clouds gather, thick and cool; the rain falls gently, wide and slow.

sentences.zh: 雨我公田，遂及我私。

translations.literal: It rains on our public fields, and then reaches our private fields."
translations.idiomatic: It waters our communal fields, then reaches our private plots."

sentences.zh: 」[二] 及幽、厲昏亂，不自為政，[三]□艷用權，七子黨進，賢愚錯緒，深谷為陵。

translations.literal: "[2] When You and Li were benighted and chaotic, they did not govern themselves; [3] Baosi exercised power; the seven sons formed a faction and advanced; worthy and foolish were tangled together; deep valleys became ridges.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Under Kings You and Li, darkness and disorder prevailed and rulers ceased to govern in person; [3] Baosi wielded influence, the seven favorites formed a clique, and worthies and fools were jumbled together until the world seemed turned upside down.

sentences.zh: 故其詩云：「四國無政，不用其良。

translations.literal: Therefore its Odes says: "In the four states there is no good government; they do not use their good men."
translations.idiomatic: Hence the Poetry laments: "Throughout the four quarters there is no sound rule; the good are not employed."

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「哀今之人，胡為虺蜴？

translations.literal: "It also says: "Alas for the people of today—why are they like vipers and lizards?"
translations.idiomatic: It adds: "Pity the people of this age—why are they treated like poisonous snakes and lizards?"

sentences.zh: 」言人畏吏如虺蜴也。

translations.literal: "It means people fear officials like vipers and lizards.
translations.idiomatic: The point is that the people dread magistrates as they would venomous reptiles.

sentences.zh: [四]宗周既滅，六國並秦，坑儒泯典，僄革五等，更立郡縣，[五] 縣設令長，郡置守尉，什伍相司，封豕其民。

translations.literal: [4] When the Zhou of the west was destroyed, the six states were annexed by Qin; it buried the scholars and extinguished the canons; it drastically cut away the five ranks and instead established commanderies and counties; [5] counties had magistrates and elders, commanderies had governors and commandants; the ten-and-five households watched one another; it treated its people like a great boar.
translations.idiomatic: [4] After Western Zhou fell and the six states were swallowed by Qin, the First Emperor buried scholars alive and burned the classics, abolished the feudal grades, and replaced them with commanderies and counties; [5] each county had a magistrate, each commandery a governor and commandant, and the mutual-surveillance groups of households penned the people in like hogs for the slaughter.

sentences.zh: [六]大漢受命，雖未復古，然克慎庶官，蠲苛救敝，悅以濟難，撫而循之。

translations.literal: [6] When the great Han received the mandate, although it did not fully restore antiquity, yet it was able to be careful with the multitude of offices, abolish harshness and rescue what was flawed, please the people to cross difficulties, and soothe and follow them.
translations.idiomatic: [6] When Han received Heaven's charge it did not fully revive the old order, yet it was scrupulous about offices, lifted oppressive laws, and nursed the realm through hardship with kindness and restraint.

sentences.zh: 至於文、景，天下康乂。

translations.literal: As for Wen and Jing, the realm was peaceful and well-governed.
translations.idiomatic: By the reigns of Emperors Wen and Jing the empire was secure and orderly.

sentences.zh: 誠由玄靖寬柔，克慎官人故也。

translations.literal: Truly it was because of profound quietness, lenient softness, and being able to be careful in appointing men.
translations.idiomatic: This was due to their deep calm, their gentle rule, and their care in choosing officials.

sentences.zh: 降及宣帝，興於仄陋，綜核名實，知時所病，刺史守相，輒親引見，考察言行，信賞必罰。

translations.literal: Descending to Emperor Xuan, who rose from a humble corner, he comprehensively examined names and realities, knew the ills of the times, and for governors and shepherds and ministers he would personally summon and interview them, investigate their words and deeds, and in rewards and punishments was trustworthy and certain.
translations.idiomatic: Down to Emperor Xuan, who had risen from obscurity, who matched names to deeds and grasped what ailed the age—when he dealt with regional inspectors, governors, and chancellors he would call them in himself, weigh word against deed, and never waver on reward or punishment.

sentences.zh: 帝乃歎曰：「民所以安而無怨者，政平吏良也。

translations.literal: The Emperor then sighed, saying: "The reason the people are peaceful and without resentment is that government is even and officials are good.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once sighed and said, "The people live untroubled and without complaint because policy is fair and the magistrates are good.

sentences.zh: 與我共此者，其唯良二千石乎！

translations.literal: Who shares this with me can only be the good two-thousand-dan!"
translations.idiomatic: Who truly shares this burden with me if not worthy governors?"

sentences.zh: 」以為吏數變易，則下不安業；

translations.literal: "He thought that if officials were frequently changed, then those below would not settle to their occupations;
translations.idiomatic: He believed that if magistrates were rotated too often, the people could not settle to their trades;

sentences.zh: 久於其事，則民服教化。

translations.literal: if they long remained at their tasks, then the people submitted to transformation by instruction.
translations.idiomatic: if they stayed long in one post, the people would accept moral instruction.

sentences.zh: 其有政理者，輒以璽書勉勵，增秩賜金，或爵至關內侯，公卿缺則以次用之。

translations.literal: As for those who had good order in government, he would with a seal-letter encourage them, increase their rank and bestow gold, or ennoble them to marquis within the passes; when dukes and ministers were lacking, he would use them in order.
translations.idiomatic: Where a man governed well, an edict of praise would go out, his salary rank was raised, gold was granted, sometimes he was ennobled as a marquis within the passes, and when a seat opened among the highest ministers he was promoted in due order.

sentences.zh: 是以吏稱其職，人安其業。

translations.literal: Therefore officials were called adequate to their posts, and people were peaceful in their occupations.
translations.idiomatic: Thus each office was filled by a man who matched it, and the people could live by their work.

sentences.zh: 漢世良吏，於茲為盛，故能降來儀之瑞，建中興之功。

translations.literal: The good officials of the Han, at this time were most abundant; therefore it was able to bring down the omens of the phoenixes' coming and establish the merit of restoration.
translations.idiomatic: Never was Han's roll of able magistrates fuller than then; that is how the omens of the phoenix were drawn down and the mid-restoration was achieved.

sentences.zh: [七] 注[一]尚書□陶謨之詞也。

translations.literal: [7] Note [1] These are words from the "Gao Yao Mo" in the Documents.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Gloss [1]: these lines come from the "Counsels of Gao Yao" in the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 惠，愛也。

translations.literal: Hui means love.
translations.idiomatic: "Hui" means loving-kindness.

sentences.zh: 黎，觿也。

translations.literal: Li means the black-haired.
translations.idiomatic: "Li" denotes the common people (the black-haired multitude).

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩小雅也。

translations.literal: Note [2] It is an ode in the Minor Odes of the Poetry.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: from the "Minor Odes" of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 渰，陰雲也。

translations.literal: Yin means yin clouds.
translations.idiomatic: "Yin" here means lowering clouds.

sentences.zh: 淒淒，雲興貌。

translations.literal: Qiqie is the appearance of clouds rising.
translations.idiomatic: "Qiqie" describes the massing of clouds.

sentences.zh: 祁，徐也。

translations.literal: Qi means slow.
translations.idiomatic: "Qi" means slow or gentle.

sentences.zh: 言陰陽和，風雨時，先雨公田，乃及私田。

translations.literal: It means yin and yang were harmonious and wind and rain came in season; first it rained on the public fields, then reached the private fields.
translations.idiomatic: The sense is that yin and yang were in harmony, the seasons of wind and rain were right, rain fell first on the communal fields and only afterward on private plots.

sentences.zh: 注[三]詩小雅刺幽王曰：「不自為政，卒勞百姓。」

translations.literal: Note [3] The Minor Odes of the Poetry satirizes King You, saying: "He does not govern himself, and in the end toils the hundred surnames."
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: the "Minor Odes" rebuke King You with the line, "He will not rule in person, and the common people bear the cost."

sentences.zh: 注[四]□艷謂□姒也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Baoyan means Baosi.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: "Baoyan" refers to the consort Baosi.

sentences.zh: 艷，色美也。

translations.literal: Yan means beautiful in color.
translations.idiomatic: "Yan" means dazzling beauty.

sentences.zh: 七子皆□姒之親黨，謂皇甫為卿士，仲允為膳夫，家伯為宰，番為司徒，蹶為趣馬，棸子為內史，楀為師氏也。

translations.literal: The seven sons were all Baosi's kinsmen and faction: Huangfu as Minister of Multitudes, Zhong Yun as Steward, Jiabo as Grand Steward, Fan as Minister of Education, Jue as Master of the Horse, Zouzi as Inner Scribe, and Yu as Master of the Guard.
translations.idiomatic: The "seven sons" were Baosi's relatives and clique: Huangfu as Minister of Multitudes, Zhong Yun as chief steward, Jiabo as grand steward, Fan as Minister of Education, Jue as Master of the Horse, Zouzi as inner scribe, and Yu as Master of the Guard.

sentences.zh: 厲王淫於色，七子皆用，言妻黨盛也。

translations.literal: King Li was dissolute in sex; the seven sons were all used; it speaks of the consort's faction being strong.
translations.idiomatic: King Li abandoned himself to women; all seven favorites held office—an image of in-laws dominating the court.

sentences.zh: 四國，四方之國也。

translations.literal: The four states are the states of the four directions.
translations.idiomatic: "The four states" means the regions in every quarter.

sentences.zh: 虺蜴之性，見人則走，哀今之人皆如是，傷時政事。

translations.literal: The nature of vipers and lizards is to run when they see people; alas for the people of today all being thus—it wounds the times and government.
translations.idiomatic: Vipers and lizards flee at the sight of man; to say "alas for the people today" likens them to such creatures—an indictment of the age and its administration.

sentences.zh: 見詩小雅。

translations.literal: See the Minor Odes of the Poetry.
translations.idiomatic: See the "Minor Odes" of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 番音方元反。

translations.literal: Fan is read fang yuan fan.
translations.idiomatic: The name Fan is read with initial f- (fan yuan fan).

sentences.zh: 棸音側流反。

translations.literal: Zou is read ce liu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Zou is read ce liu fan.

sentences.zh: 楀音記禹反。

translations.literal: Yu is read ji yu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Yu is read ji yu fan.

sentences.zh: 注[五]僄，削也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Piao means to pare away.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: "piao" means to slice away or abolish.

sentences.zh: 五等謂諸侯。

translations.literal: The five ranks mean the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: The "five ranks" are the grades of feudal nobility.

sentences.zh: 注[六]史記，商鞅為秦定變法之令，令人什伍而相牧司，犯禁相連坐，不告奸者□斬。

translations.literal: Note [6] The Records of the Historian: Shang Yang for Qin established the laws of reform, ordering people in groups of ten and five to watch one another; those who violated prohibitions were jointly liable; those who did not report wrongdoing were [to be cut at] the waist.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: the Shiji records that Shang Yang's Qin reforms grouped households in fives and tens for mutual surveillance and collective punishment, with death for failure to inform.

sentences.zh: 楊雄長楊賦曰「秦窫窳其士，封豕其人」也。

translations.literal: Yang Xiong's "Rhapsody on the Long Hunt" says "Qin made its knights cowardly and its people like great boars."
translations.idiomatic: As Yang Xiong wrote in his "Rhapsody on the Long Hunt," "Qin made its warriors craven and its people swine to be penned."

sentences.zh: 注[七]宣帝時鳳皇五至，因以紀年。

translations.literal: Note [7] In Emperor Xuan's time phoenixes came five times, and thereby years were recorded.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7]: under Emperor Xuan the phoenix appeared five times, and reign titles commemorated it.

sentences.zh: 漢初至今，三百餘載，俗浸雕敝，巧偽滋萌，下飾其詐，上肆其殘。

translations.literal: From the beginning of Han until now, more than three hundred years; customs have gradually been carved and worn; craftiness and falsehood have sprouted; those below adorn their deceit, those above indulge their cruelty.
translations.idiomatic: From the founding of Han to the present—well over three hundred years—customs have eroded, fraud has multiplied, inferiors varnish their deceit, and superiors indulge cruelty.

sentences.zh: 典城百里，轉動無常，各懷一切，莫慮長久。

translations.literal: Those who hold a walled city of a hundred li turn and shift without constancy; each harbors a single-minded expedient and none considers what will last long.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrates over a hundred li of territory are rotated without pattern; each pursues short-term fixes and none plans for the long term.

sentences.zh: 謂殺害不辜為威風，聚斂整辨為賢能，以理己安民為劣弱，以奉法循理為不化。

translations.literal: They call killing the innocent to be awe-inspiring authority, gathering wealth and being orderly to be worthy ability, ordering oneself and pacifying the people to be weak and inferior, and following the law and adhering to principle to be unresponsive to transformation.
translations.idiomatic: They take the slaughter of innocents for "awe," tax squeezing and tidy accounts for "talent," self-restraint and care for the people for "weakness," and faithful adherence to law for "obstinacy."

sentences.zh: 髡鉗之戮，生於睚眥；

translations.literal: The punishment of shaving the head and iron collar arises from a sidelong glance;
translations.idiomatic: Shaving and the cangue follow from a sideways glance;

sentences.zh: 覆屍之禍，成於喜怒。

translations.literal: the disaster of corpses overturned is completed by joy and anger.
translations.idiomatic: bodies pile in ditches because of a mood swung between pleasure and rage.

sentences.zh: 視民如寇讎，稅之如豺虎。

translations.literal: They look on the people as bandits and enemies, and tax them like wolves and tigers.
translations.idiomatic: They eye the commoners as foes to be plundered and squeeze them like wolves and tigers.

sentences.zh: [一]監司項背相望，[二]與同疾疢，見非不舉，聞惡不察，觀政於亭傳，責成於開月，[三]言善不稱德，論功不據實，虛誕者獲譽，拘檢者離毀。

translations.literal: [1] Supervisors are as numerous as backs and necks in a line; [2] they share the same sores, see wrong and do not report, hear evil and do not investigate; they observe government at post-stations and demand results within a full cycle of months; [3] they praise goodness without reference to virtue, discuss merit without grounding in fact; the empty and boastful gain reputation, the restrained and careful meet slander.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Inspectors crowd the road one behind another; [2] they nurse the same sores, pass over faults they see and ignore crimes they hear, judge administration from relay inns, and demand yearly accounts; [3] they celebrate "virtue" without moral substance and credit "merit" without evidence, so the hollow flourish while the conscientious are torn down.

sentences.zh: [四]或因罪而引高，或色斯以求名。

translations.literal: [4] Some because of crime withdraw to loftiness, or by observing complexion seek a name.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Some flee office in the name of principle, or study others' faces to win a reputation.

sentences.zh: [五]州宰不覆，競共辟召，踴躍升騰，超等踰匹。

translations.literal: [5] Prefects do not review; they compete in summoning; they leap and ascend, exceeding grades and leaping over peers.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Governors skip investigation and race to recruit favorites who vault past proper rank.

sentences.zh: 或考奏捕案，而亡不受罪，會赦行賂，復見洗滌。

translations.literal: Sometimes they are impeached and arrested and investigated, yet flee and do not accept punishment; when an amnesty comes they offer bribes and again are washed clean.
translations.idiomatic: Men impeached and bound for trial bolt and refuse sentence, then bribe their way clear at the next general amnesty.

sentences.zh: 朱紫同色，清濁不分。

translations.literal: Crimson and purple are the same color; clear and turbid are not distinguished.
translations.idiomatic: Vermilion and violet blur together; the clear cannot be told from the murky.

sentences.zh: 故使奸猾枉濫，輕忽去就，拜除如流，缺動百數。

translations.literal: Therefore it causes the crafty and slippery to be perverted and excessive, lightly to disregard going and coming; appointments and removals flow like a current, vacancies stir by the hundreds.
translations.idiomatic: So the cunning grow brazen, treat office as a revolving door, and appointments pour out while hundreds of posts stand empty.

sentences.zh: 鄉官部吏，職斯祿薄，[六]車馬衣服，一出於民，廉者取足，貪者充家，特選橫調，[七]紛紛不絕，送迎煩費，損政傷民。

translations.literal: Village officers and department clerks, their duties are thus and their salary thin; [6] carriages, horses, and clothing all come from the people; the incorrupt take enough, the greedy fill their households; special levies and arbitrary exactions [7] come endlessly; sending off and welcoming are vexing and costly, harming government and wounding the people.
translations.idiomatic: Grass-roots clerks earn little yet must fund horses, carriages, and dress from the populace—honest men scrape by, the greedy enrich their kin, while special surcharges never cease and the cost of escorting every new magistrate wastes both treasury and people.

sentences.zh: 和氣未洽，□眚不消，咎皆在此。

translations.literal: Harmonious qi has not penetrated; calamities and portents have not vanished; the blame is all here.
translations.idiomatic: Harmony never takes hold, omens of ill do not lift, and the fault lies here.

sentences.zh: 今之墨綬，猶古之諸侯，[八]拜爵王庭，輿服有庸，[九]而齊於匹豎，叛命避負，非所以崇憲明理，惠育元元也。

translations.literal: Now the black ribbon is still the feudal lord of antiquity; [8] they receive rank at the royal court and carriages and robes have their norm; [9] yet they are equated with common peddlers, betraying orders and fleeing responsibility—this is not the way to exalt the statutes, clarify principle, and kindly nurture the primal people.
translations.idiomatic: Today's black-sashed magistrate is the heir of the old feudal lord: [8] he receives his charge in the palace and his equipage is regulated; [9] yet he is classed with street hucksters who abandon duty and bolt—hardly how to honor the law, make reason plain, or nurture the common folk.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為守相長吏，惠和有顯□者，可就增秩，勿使移徙，非父母喪不得去官。

translations.literal: Your subject foolishly thinks that for shepherds, ministers, and elders, those who are kindly and harmonious and have evident merit, one may increase their rank and not allow them to be transferred; except for the mourning of parents they may not leave office.
translations.idiomatic: I would urge that governors and magistrates who govern gently and show clear merit receive higher rank and not be shuffled elsewhere, and that none may resign save for a parent's funeral.

sentences.zh: 其不從法禁，不式王命，錮之終身，[一０]雖會赦令，不得齒列。

translations.literal: Those who do not follow the prohibitions of the law and do not model the king's commands, imprison them for life; [10] although there may be an amnesty decree, they may not be ranked among officials.
translations.idiomatic: Whoever defies statute and ignores the sovereign's command should be barred for life—even under amnesty he must never again hold office.

sentences.zh: 若被劾奏，亡不就法者，徙家邊郡，以懲其後。

translations.literal: If impeached and summoned and they flee and do not submit to the law, move their families to the border commanderies to warn those who come after.
translations.idiomatic: If a man under impeachment flees rather than answer the law, exile his household to the frontier as a warning to others.

sentences.zh: 鄉部親民之吏，皆用儒生清白任從政者，[一一]寬其負筭，[一二]增其秩祿，吏職滿歲，宰府州郡乃得辟舉。

translations.literal: The clerks of villages and departments who are close to the people should all use Confucian scholars who are pure and white and fit to handle government; [11] relax their tax assessments; [12] increase their rank and salary; when a full year of clerical service is complete, only then may the minister's office, the province, or the commandery summon and recommend them.
translations.idiomatic: All village and precinct officers who touch the people should be chosen from clean-living literati fit for public duty; [11] lighten their poll taxes; [12] raise their pay; and after a full year of service let only the capital bureaus, provinces, or commanderies recruit them.

sentences.zh: 如此，威福之路塞，虛偽之端絕，送迎之役損，賦斂之源息。

translations.literal: If so, the road to power and blessing is blocked, the beginnings of emptiness and falsehood are cut off, the labor of sending off and welcoming is reduced, and the sources of levies are stilled.
translations.idiomatic: Then the path to arbitrary power closes, fraud dies, escort duty shrinks, and exactions dry up.

sentences.zh: 循理之吏，得成其化；

translations.literal: Officials who follow principle will be able to complete their transformation;
translations.idiomatic: Magistrates who keep to principle can finish the moral transformation of their districts;

sentences.zh: 率土之民，各寧其所。

translations.literal: the people within the bounds of the soil will each be peaceful in their place.
translations.idiomatic: and everyone under Heaven will rest secure where they belong.

sentences.zh: 追配文、宣中興之軌，[一三] 流光垂祚，永世不刊。

translations.literal: Tracing and matching the track of the restoration of Wen and Xuan, [13] flowing radiance will extend blessings, never to be carved away for ages.
translations.idiomatic: By echoing the mid-Han restoration of Emperors Wen and Xuan, [13] your glory will spread and your heirs will long enjoy an unshakable mandate.

sentences.zh: 注[一]國語曰：「□丹廷見令尹子常，與之語，問畜貨聚*

translations.literal: Note [1] The Discourses of the States says: "[Dan] of Shen appeared in court to see Grand Minister Zichang, and spoke with him, asking about hoarding goods and gathering*
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Guoyu records that Shen Dan visited Minister Zichang at court and asked how he stored wealth and stockpiled

translations.literal: (there)
translations.idiomatic: (there)

translations.literal: **[horses]*.
translations.idiomatic: horses.

sentences.zh: 歸以語其弟曰：『楚其亡乎？

translations.literal: Returning, he told his younger brother, saying: "Will Chu perhaps perish?
translations.idiomatic: On returning he told his younger brother, "Chu is surely doomed.

sentences.zh: 吾見令尹如餓獸豺虎焉，殆必亡者也。

translations.literal: I see the grand minister like a starving beast, wolves and tigers; he is almost certainly one who must bring ruin."
translations.idiomatic: I looked at the minister: he is a starving wolf or tiger—such a man will destroy the state."

sentences.zh: 』」注[二]項背相望謂前後相顧也。

translations.literal: '" Note [2] Xiang bei xiang wang means looking at one another front and back.
translations.idiomatic: End quote." Note [2]: "backs and necks in a line" means craning to look ahead and behind.

sentences.zh: 背音輩。

translations.literal: Bei is read like bei (generation).
translations.idiomatic: The character bei is read like "generation" (bei).

sentences.zh: 注[三]開，匝也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Kai means a full circuit.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: "kai" means a full turn or cycle.

sentences.zh: 謂一歲。

translations.literal: It means one year.
translations.idiomatic: Here it means one year.

sentences.zh: 注[四]離，遭也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Li means to encounter.
translations.idiomatic: Note [4]: "li" means to meet with (suffer).

sentences.zh: 注[五]因罪潛遁，以求高尚之名也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Because of crime they slip away to seek a lofty name.
translations.idiomatic: Note [5]: some hide from prosecution while posing as high-minded recluses.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「色斯舉矣。

translations.literal: The Analects says: "At a change of countenance he rose."
translations.idiomatic: The Analects says, "When the countenance changed, he rose."

sentences.zh: 」言觀前人之顏色也。

translations.literal: "It means observing the complexion of those before one.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: watching the ruler's mood to decide when to flatter or withdraw.

sentences.zh: 注[六]斯，賤也。

translations.literal: Note [6] Si means lowly.
translations.idiomatic: Note [6]: in another gloss "si" means low or mean.

sentences.zh: 注[七]調，征也。

translations.literal: Note [7] Diao means to levy.
translations.idiomatic: Note [7]: "diao" means to impose levies.

sentences.zh: 注[八]墨綬謂令長，即古子男之國也。

translations.literal: Note [8] The black ribbon means magistrates and elders, that is, the states of the ancient viscounts and barons.
translations.idiomatic: Note [8]: the black sash marks county magistrates—the modern counterparts of the old viscount and baron domains.

sentences.zh: 注[九]庸，常也。

translations.literal: Note [9] Yong means constant.
translations.idiomatic: Note [9]: "yong" here means regular or customary.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]式，用也。

translations.literal: Note [10] Shi means to use.
translations.idiomatic: Note [10]: "shi" means to apply or follow.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]任，堪也，音人林反。

translations.literal: Note [11] Ren means to be equal to; it is read ren lin fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [11]: "ren" means competent to the task; the reading is ren lin fan.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]負，欠也。

translations.literal: Note [12] Fu means to owe.
translations.idiomatic: Note [12]: "fu" means arrears or debt.

sentences.zh: 筭，口錢也。

translations.literal: Suan means mouth money.
translations.idiomatic: "Suan" denotes the poll tax on adults.

sentences.zh: 儒生未有品秩，故寬之。

translations.literal: Confucian scholars had not yet received rank and salary, therefore they were lenient toward them.
translations.idiomatic: Because literati had no formal rank, the rules eased their tax burden.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]文帝、宣帝也。

translations.literal: Note [13] Emperor Wen and Emperor Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [13]: Emperors Wen and Xuan of Han.

sentences.zh: 文帝遭呂氏難，故亦云中興。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen suffered the troubles of the Lü clan, therefore it is also called mid-restoration.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen survived the Lü usurpation, so his reign too counts among Han's restorations.

sentences.zh: 帝感其言，申下有司，考其真偽，詳所施行。

translations.literal: The Emperor was moved by his words, extended the order downward to the responsible offices, examined their truth and falsehood, and detailed what was to be implemented.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took his advice to heart, ordered the bureaus to verify every point, and spell out what to put into practice.

sentences.zh: 雄之所言，皆明達政體，而宦豎□權，終不能用。

translations.literal: What Xiong said all clarified the substance of government, but the eunuchs monopolized power, and in the end they could not employ him.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong's every word showed a grasp of statecraft, yet eunuchs held the real power and his reforms never took hold.

sentences.zh: 自是選代交互，令長月易，迎新送舊，勞擾無已，或官寺空曠，無人案事，每選部劇，乃至逃亡。

translations.literal: From this time appointments and replacements crossed back and forth; magistrates and elders changed monthly; welcoming the new and sending off the old brought toil and disturbance without end; sometimes official compounds stood empty with no one to handle cases; whenever the Board chose a difficult post, men even fled.
translations.idiomatic: After that, magistrates were shuffled incessantly—some counties saw a new face every month, escorts never rested, yamen gates stood empty with no one hearing suits, and when the ministry posted a "hard" county, candidates simply ran away.

sentences.zh: 永建三年，京師、漢陽地皆震裂，水泉湧出。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yongjian, the capital and Hanyang both had the earth crack open and springs gush forth.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongjian 3 the ground split in the capital and in Hanyang, and water burst from new fissures.

sentences.zh: 四年，司、冀復有大水。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, Si and Ji again had great floods.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Si and Ji provinces were again inundated.

sentences.zh: 雄推較□異，以為下人有逆上之征，[一]又上疏言：「宜密為備，以俟不虞。

translations.literal: Xiong compared and calculated the anomalies, thinking that below there were signs of the inferior rebelling against the superior; [1] he again submitted a memorial saying: "It is fitting secretly to make preparations against the unexpected."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong collated these portents as omens of subjects turning against their rulers; [1] he memorialized again: "You should quietly ready the realm for whatever may come."

sentences.zh: 」尋而青、冀、楊州盜賊連發，數年之閒，海內擾亂。

translations.literal: " Soon thereafter bandits broke out in succession in Qing, Ji, and Yang provinces; within several years the realm within the seas was disturbed and chaotic.
translations.idiomatic: Soon rebels erupted across Qing, Ji, and Yang, and within a few years the empire was in turmoil.

sentences.zh: 其後天下大赦，賊雖頗解，而官猶無備，流叛之餘，數月復起。

translations.literal: Afterward the realm had a great amnesty; although the bandits somewhat dispersed, yet the government still had no preparations; the remnants of the wandering rebels, within several months, rose again.
translations.idiomatic: A general amnesty followed and the uprisings eased, but the court still made no preparations, so the surviving rebels rekindled the war within months.

sentences.zh: 雄與僕射郭虔共上疏，以為「寇賊連年，死亡太半，一人犯法，舉宗腢亡。

translations.literal: Xiong together with Vice Director Guo Qian jointly submitted a memorial, thinking that "for years the bandits have ravaged the land; more than half have died; one man breaks the law and whole clans flee in bonds."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong and Vice Director Guo Qian jointly urged: "Years of banditry have halved the population; a single crime now drives entire lineages into outlawry."

sentences.zh: 宜及其尚微，開令改悔。

translations.literal: It is fitting while they are still small to open a way for them to reform and repent.
translations.idiomatic: Strike while the evil is still small: offer them a path to repent.

sentences.zh: 若告黨與者，聽除其罪；

translations.literal: If they report their accomplices, allow their crimes to be remitted;
translations.idiomatic: Grant amnesty to anyone who exposes his accomplices;

sentences.zh: 能誅斬者，明加其賞」。

translations.literal: for those who can kill and behead, clearly add to their reward."
translations.idiomatic: and pay a clear bounty to anyone who captures or kills the leaders."

sentences.zh: 書奏，並不省。

translations.literal: When the document was submitted, neither was examined.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial went up and sank without a trace.

sentences.zh: 注[一]天鏡經曰：「大水自平地出，破山殺人，其國有兵。」

translations.literal: Note [1] The Scripture of the Mirror of Heaven says: "When great water rises from level ground, breaks mountains, and kills people, that state will have war."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Tianjing jing reads, "Floods that burst from flat ground, shatter hills, and drown people foretell war for that kingdom."

sentences.zh: 又上言：「宜崇經術，繕修太學。

translations.literal: He again submitted words: "It is fitting to honor the classics and arts, and repair and build the Imperial Academy."
translations.idiomatic: He urged again: "Elevate classical learning and rebuild the Imperial Academy."

sentences.zh: 」帝從之。

translations.literal: " The Emperor followed this.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 陽嘉元年，太學新成，詔試明經者補弟子，增甲乙之科，員各十人。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yangjia, when the Imperial Academy was newly completed, he issued an edict that those who passed the examination on the classics should be appointed disciples, increasing the grades of jia and yi, ten men for each.
translations.idiomatic: In Yangjia 1, when the new academy stood finished, an edict opened places for classicists who passed the exam as disciples, with ten slots each in the jia and yi ranks.

sentences.zh: 除京師及郡國耆儒年六十以上為郎、舍人、諸王國郎者百三十八人。

translations.literal: He removed from the capital and the commanderies and kingdoms venerable Confucians aged sixty and above to be Gentlemen, housemen, or Gentlemen to the various princely kingdoms—one hundred thirty-eight men.
translations.idiomatic: He appointed a hundred thirty-eight gray-bearded scholars of sixty or more from the capital and the provinces as Gentlemen, palace attendants, or princely householders.

sentences.zh: 雄又上言：「郡國孝廉，古之貢士，出則宰民，宣協風教。

translations.literal: Xiong again submitted words: "The commandery and kingdom filial and incorrupt are the tribute scholars of antiquity; when they go out they shepherd the people and should harmonize with custom and instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong added: "The filial-and-incorrupt nominees from the provinces are the old tribute scholars; once in office they govern the people and must embody local mores and moral teaching.

sentences.zh: 若其面牆，則無所施用。

translations.literal: If they are like a wall to the face, then there is nowhere to apply them.
translations.idiomatic: If they are blank as a wall facing you, they are useless in office.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰『四十不惑』，禮稱『強仕』。

translations.literal: Confucius said 'At forty one is free from doubts,' and the Rites call forty 'the age of strength for office.'
translations.idiomatic: The Master said, "At forty doubts cease," and the ritual canon calls forty the age fit for full duty.

sentences.zh: 請自今孝廉年不滿四十，不得察舉，皆先詣公府，諸生試家法，[一]文吏課箋奏，副之端門，練其虛實，以觀異能，以美風俗。

translations.literal: Please from now on not investigate or recommend filial and incorrupt who are not yet forty years of age; all should first go to the public offices; students shall be tested on their house learning, [1] clerks shall be examined on memorial drafts; post the duplicates at the Duan Gate, drill their truth and falsehood, observe their unusual abilities, and thereby beautify custom.
translations.idiomatic: Henceforth, he proposed, no one under forty should be nominated as filial and incorrupt; each candidate must first present himself at the capital bureaus—students tested on their master's school of thought, [1] clerks on petition style—with copies posted at the Duan Gate so examiners could sift substance from show and thereby improve public morals.

sentences.zh: 有不承科令者，正其罪法。

translations.literal: Those who do not comply with the statutes and orders, correct their crime according to the law.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who flouts the regulations should be punished under statute.

sentences.zh: 若有茂才異行，自可不拘年齒。

translations.literal: If there are outstanding talent and unusual conduct, naturally one need not be bound by age.
translations.idiomatic: Men of conspicuous genius may of course be exempted from the age rule.

sentences.zh: 」帝從之，於是班下郡國。

translations.literal: " The Emperor followed this, and thereupon issued it to the commanderies and kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted the plan and promulgated it to every province.

sentences.zh: 明年，有廣陵孝廉徐淑，[二]年未及舉，台郎疑而詰之。

translations.literal: The next year there was a filial and incorrupt from Guangling, Xu Shu, [2] whose years had not yet reached recommendation; the Gentlemen of the terrace doubted and questioned him.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Guangling nominated Xu Shu as filial and incorrupt [2] though he was under forty; the palace Gentlemen challenged him.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「詔書曰『有如顏回、子奇，不拘年齒』，[三]是故本郡以臣充選。

translations.literal: He replied, saying: "The edict said 'If there be such as Yan Hui or Ziqi, do not be bound by age,' [3] therefore my original commandery sent me as the choice."
translations.idiomatic: He answered: "The edict says, 'For a Yan Hui or a Ziqi, waive the age limit,' [3] so my commandery put me forward."

sentences.zh: 」郎不能屈。

translations.literal: " The Gentleman could not bend him.
translations.idiomatic: The examiner could not refute him.

sentences.zh: 雄詰之曰：「昔顏回聞一知十，孝廉聞一知幾邪？

translations.literal: Xiong questioned him, saying: "In antiquity Yan Hui heard one and knew ten; does the filial and incorrupt hear one and know how many?"
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong shot back: "Yan Hui heard one thing and understood ten; how many does a filial-and-incorrupt understand from one?"

sentences.zh: 」淑無以對，乃譴□郡。

translations.literal: " Shu had nothing with which to reply, and thereupon was reprimanded and sent back to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Shu had no answer and was sent home in disgrace.

sentences.zh: 於是濟陰太守胡廣等十餘人皆坐謬舉免黜，唯汝南陳蕃、穎川李膺、下邳陳球等三十餘人得拜郎中。

translations.literal: Thereupon more than ten persons including the Grand Administrator of Jiyin, Hu Guang, all sat for erroneous recommendation and were dismissed; only thirty-odd such as Chen Fan of Runan, Li Ying of Yingchuan, and Chen Qiu of Xiapi received appointment as Gentlemen of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: More than ten governors, Hu Guang of Jiyin among them, lost office for bad nominations, while only some thirty—including Chen Fan of Runan, Li Ying of Yingchuan, and Chen Qiu of Xiapi—passed and became Gentlemen of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 自是牧守畏栗，莫敢輕舉。

translations.literal: From this shepherds and guards trembled with fear and none dared lightly to recommend.
translations.idiomatic: After that provincial chiefs nominated candidates with trembling care.

sentences.zh: 迄於永*

translations.literal: Down to Yong*
translations.idiomatic: Down to the Yong

translations.literal: (jia)
translations.idiomatic: (jia)

sentences.zh: **[□]*，察選清平，多得其人。

translations.literal: **[lacuna]* era, the inspections and selections were pure and even, and for the most part they obtained the right men.
translations.idiomatic: jia era, selections stayed fair and the right men usually won office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]儒有一家之學，故稱家*[法]*。

translations.literal: Note [1] Confucians have the learning of a single house, therefore it is called house learning.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: each classicist follows one master's tradition, hence "house learning."

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰「淑字伯進，廣陵海西人也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Xie Cheng's book says: "Shu, courtesy name Bojin, was a man of Haixi in Guangling.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: Xie Cheng's history names him Xu Shu, courtesy Bojin, a native of Haixi in Guangling.

sentences.zh: 寬裕博雅，好學樂道。

translations.literal: He was magnanimous, learned, and fond of study and the Way.
translations.idiomatic: He was broad-minded, erudite, and devoted to learning.

sentences.zh: 隨父慎在京師，鑽孟氏易、春秋、公羊、禮記、周官。

translations.literal: Following his father Shen in the capital, he drilled into the Meng family Changes, the Spring and Autumn, the Gongyang, the Record of Rites, and the Offices of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: While with his father Shen at court he mastered the Meng Changes, the Spring and Autumn Annals, the Gongyang commentary, the Book of Rites, and the Rites of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 善誦太公六韜，交接英雄，常有壯志。

translations.literal: He was good at reciting the Grand Duke's Six Secret Teachings, associated with heroes, and constantly had lofty ambitions.
translations.idiomatic: He could recite the Grand Duke's Six Tactics, moved among men of valor, and nursed high ambitions.

sentences.zh: 舉茂才，除勃海修令，遷琅邪都尉」也。

translations.literal: He was recommended as outstanding talent, was appointed magistrate of Xiu in Bohai, and was promoted to commandant of Langye."
translations.idiomatic: Later he was nominated as maocai, served as magistrate of Xiu in Bohai, and rose to commandant of Langye."

sentences.zh: 注[三]解見順帝紀。

translations.literal: Note [3] The explanation is seen in the annals of Emperor Shun.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: see the biography of Emperor Shun.

sentences.zh: 雄又奏征海內名儒為博士，使公卿子弟為諸生。

translations.literal: Xiong again memorialized to summon famous Confucians from within the seas to be erudits, and to make the sons of dukes and ministers students.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong further asked the throne to call famous scholars from the empire as academy erudits and enroll noble heirs as students.

sentences.zh: 有志操者，加其俸祿。

translations.literal: Those who had determination and conduct, increase their salary.
translations.idiomatic: Students who showed character and resolve received higher stipends.

sentences.zh: 及汝南謝廉，河南趙建，年始十二，各能通經，雄並奏拜童子郎。

translations.literal: As for Xie Lian of Runan and Zhao Jian of Henan, who at the beginning were only twelve sui, each being able to penetrate the classics, Xiong together memorialized to appoint them as boy Gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: When Runan's Xie Lian and Henan's Zhao Jian, each twelve years old, proved their mastery of the classics, Zuo Xiong jointly recommended them as child Gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 於是負書來學，雲集京師。

translations.literal: Thereupon those bearing books came to study, cloudlike gathering at the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Scholars flocked to the capital with satchels of books.

sentences.zh: 初，帝廢為濟陰王，乳母宋娥與黃門孫程等共議立帝，帝后以娥前有謀，遂封為山陽君，邑五千戶。

translations.literal: At first, when the Emperor had been deposed as Prince of Jiyin, his wet-nurse Song E together with the Yellow Gate Sun Cheng and others jointly plotted to establish the Emperor; the Empress later, because E had earlier had a plan, thereupon enfeoffed her as Lady of Shanyang, with a fief of five thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, when the emperor was still the deposed Prince of Jiyin, his nurse Song E had joined eunuch Sun Cheng in enthroning him; the empress later rewarded her past service by enfeoffing her as Lady of Shanyang with five thousand households.

sentences.zh: 又封大將軍梁商子冀襄邑侯。

translations.literal: He also enfeoffed the Grand General Liang Shang's son Ji as marquis of Xiangyi.
translations.idiomatic: The throne also made Grand General Liang Shang's son Ji marquis of Xiangyi.

sentences.zh: 雄上封事曰：「夫裂土封侯，王制所重。

translations.literal: Xiong submitted a sealed memorial saying: "To split land and enfeoff marquises is what the royal system esteems.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong sent a sealed memorial: "Carving out territory for enfeoffment is the foundation of kingship.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝約，非劉氏不王，非有功不侯。

translations.literal: The covenant of the High Emperor: unless one is surnamed Liu one is not made king; unless one has merit one is not made marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu's iron rule was: no Liu, no kingship; no merit, no marquisate.

sentences.zh: 孝安皇帝封江京、王聖等，遂致地震之異。

translations.literal: Emperor An enfeoffed Jiang Jing, Wang Sheng, and others, and thereupon brought on the anomaly of earthquakes.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor An enfeoffed Jiang Jing, Wang Sheng, and their like, the earth answered with quakes.

sentences.zh: 永建二年，封陰謀之功，又有日食之變。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongjian, for the merit of the secret plot there was again the anomaly of a solar eclipse.
translations.idiomatic: Yongjian 2 saw rewards for the coup plotters—and another solar eclipse.

sentences.zh: 數術之士，鹹歸咎於封爵。

translations.literal: Men of numbers and arts all attributed blame to enfeoffments.
translations.idiomatic: Every omen specialist blamed the rash of new fiefs.

sentences.zh: 今青州饑虛，盜賊未息，民有乏絕，上求稟貸。

translations.literal: Now Qingzhou is hungry and empty; bandits have not yet ceased; the people are exhausted and cut off; above they beg for grain loans.
translations.idiomatic: Qingzhou is starving, rebels still roam, the people are destitute, and memorials beg for grain relief.

sentences.zh: 陛下干干勞思，以濟民為務。

translations.literal: Your Majesty toils and worries, taking succor of the people as the task.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty burns midnight oil to save the commoners.

sentences.zh: 宜循古法，寧靜無為，以求天意，以消□異。

translations.literal: It is fitting to follow the ancient methods, be tranquil and without action, in order to seek Heaven's mind and thereby dispel anomalies.
translations.idiomatic: You should return to the old ways—quiet rule—to win Heaven's favor and lift these portents.

sentences.zh: 誠不宜追錄小恩，虧失大典。

translations.literal: Truly it is not fitting to pursue recording small kindnesses and thereby injure the great canons.
translations.idiomatic: It is wrong to repay petty favors at the cost of the great statutes.

sentences.zh: 」帝不聽。

translations.literal: " The Emperor did not listen.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor would not hear of it.

sentences.zh: 雄復諫曰：「臣聞人君莫不好忠正而惡讒諛，然而歷世之患，莫不以忠正得罪，讒諛蒙幸者，蓋聽忠難，從諛易也。

translations.literal: Xiong again remonstrated, saying: "Your subject has heard that no ruler of men does not love loyalty and uprightness and hate slander and flattery, yet the calamity of successive ages is that none fail to have loyalty and uprightness meet with punishment while slander and flattery receive favor—because listening to loyalty is hard and following flattery is easy.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong protested again: "Every ruler claims to love straight speech and hate flattery, yet age after age loyal men are ruined while toadies thrive—because truth is hard to hear and flattery easy to swallow."

sentences.zh: 夫刑罪，人情之所甚惡；

translations.literal: As for punishments and crimes, they are what human feeling greatly hates;
translations.idiomatic: Punishment is what every heart dreads;

sentences.zh: 貴寵，人情之所甚欲。

translations.literal: noble favor is what human feeling greatly desires.
translations.idiomatic: while favor is what every heart craves.

sentences.zh: 是以時俗為忠者少，而習諛者多。

translations.literal: Therefore in the customs of the times those who act as loyalists are few while those who practice flattery are many.
translations.idiomatic: So the age produces few who dare be loyal and many who learn to flatter.

sentences.zh: 故令人主數聞其美，稀知其過，迷而不悟，至於危亡。

translations.literal: Therefore it causes the lord of men repeatedly to hear his own excellence and rarely to know his faults, to be lost and not awaken, until danger and ruin.
translations.idiomatic: The ruler hears endless praise of himself, seldom his faults, drifts in a fog, and slides toward ruin.

sentences.zh: 臣伏見詔書顧念阿母舊德宿恩，欲特加顯賞。

translations.literal: Your subject has humbly seen in an edict that Your Majesty thinks back on the wet-nurse's old virtue and long-standing grace and wishes especially to add conspicuous reward.
translations.idiomatic: I note that an edict would reward your nurse's long service with extraordinary honors.

sentences.zh: 案尚書故事，無乳母爵邑之制，唯先帝時阿母王聖為野王君。

translations.literal: Examining the precedents of the Masters of Writing, there is no system of ennobling wet-nurses with towns; only under a former emperor did the wet-nurse Wang Sheng become Lady of Yewang.
translations.idiomatic: The archives show no precedent for enfeoffing nurses; only once before did Wang Sheng become Lady of Yewang.

sentences.zh: 聖造生讒賊廢立之禍，生為天下所咀嚼，死為海內所歡快。

translations.literal: Sheng gave birth to the disaster of slanderous villains' deposing and establishing; in life she was chewed by all under Heaven; in death she was rejoiced at within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: That woman spawned the cabal that deposed emperors—alive she was gnawed over by the empire, dead she was cheered across the realm.

sentences.zh: 桀、紂貴為天子，而庸僕羞與為比者，以其無義也。

translations.literal: Jie and Zhou were honored as Sons of Heaven, yet common servants were ashamed to be compared with them, because they lacked righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and Zhou sat on the throne, yet ordinary slaves scorned their name for want of righteousness.

sentences.zh: 夷、齊賤為匹夫，而王侯爭與為伍者，以其有德也。

translations.literal: Yi and Qi were low as commoners, yet kings and marquises vied to be their companions, because they had virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Boyi and Shuqi were mere commoners, yet princes competed to stand with them for their virtue.

sentences.zh: 今阿母躬蹈約儉，以身率下，腢僚蒸庶，莫不向風，而與王聖並同爵號，懼違本操，失其常願。

translations.literal: Now the wet-nurse personally treads restraint and frugality, taking her body to lead those below; colleagues and the multitude all turn to the wind; yet to give her the same noble title as Wang Sheng fears that it violates her original conduct and loses her constant wish.
translations.idiomatic: Your nurse lives modestly and sets a frugal example; officials and people all take her cue—yet to match Wang Sheng's title may betray her own principles and what she truly wants.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為凡人之心，理不相遠，其所不安，古今一也。

translations.literal: Your subject foolishly thinks that the hearts of ordinary men, in principle are not far apart; what they do not feel at ease with is one in ancient and modern.
translations.idiomatic: I believe ordinary hearts are alike: what unsettles them today unsettled them yesterday.

sentences.zh: 百姓深懲王聖傾覆之禍，民萌之命，危於累卵，常懼時世復有此類。

translations.literal: The hundred surnames deeply punish Wang Sheng's overturning disaster; the lives of the people are more dangerous than piled eggs; they constantly fear that the age will again have this sort.
translations.idiomatic: The people still smart from Wang Sheng's coup; their lives hang by a thread; they dread another such hour.

sentences.zh: 怵惕之念，未離於心；

translations.literal: The thoughts of fear and caution have not left their hearts;
translations.idiomatic: Fear still gnaws at them;

sentences.zh: 恐懼之言，未絕乎口。

translations.literal: the words of fear have not ceased from their mouths.
translations.idiomatic: and frightened talk never leaves their lips.

sentences.zh: 乞如前議，歲以千萬給奉阿母，內足以盡恩愛之歡，外可不為吏民所怪。

translations.literal: Beg as in the former discussion yearly to give the wet-nurse a stipend in the tens of millions, inwardly sufficient to exhaust the joy of loving kindness, outwardly able not to be wondered at by clerks and people.
translations.idiomatic: Do as we urged before: grant her a million cash a year—enough to show affection without shocking officials and commoners.

sentences.zh: 梁冀之封，事非機急，宜過□□之運，然後平議可否。

translations.literal: The enfeoffment of Liang Ji is not a matter of urgent crisis; it is fitting to pass the period of [lacuna] movement, and afterward calmly discuss whether it may be done.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji's marquisate is no emergency; wait until this troubled cycle passes, then debate his fief in calm council.

sentences.zh: 」會復有地震、緱氏山崩之異，雄復上疏諫曰：「先帝封野王君，漢陽地震，今封山陽君而京城復震，專政在陰，其□尤大。

translations.literal: " At the time there were again the anomalies of earthquake and the collapse of Mount Gou'shi; Xiong again submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying: "When the former emperor enfeoffed the Lady of Yewang, Hanyang had an earthquake; now enfeoffing the Lady of Shanyang and the capital again has an earthquake; exclusive government lies in the yin force; its [sign] is especially great.
translations.idiomatic: Then came fresh quakes and the fall of Mount Gou'shi. Zuo Xiong wrote again: "Yewang's enfeoffment shook Hanyang; Shanyang's title now shakes the capital—yin power dominates, and the omen is grave."

sentences.zh: 臣前後瞽言封爵至重，王者可私人以財，不可以官，宜還阿母之封，以塞□異。

translations.literal: Your subject before and after blindly spoke that enfeoffment is extremely weighty; the king may privately give people wealth, but may not give them office; it is fitting to return the wet-nurse's enfeoffment in order to stop anomalies.
translations.idiomatic: I have said again and again that titles outweigh gifts: a king may enrich a favorite, not ennoble her at whim—withdraw the nurse's fief to quiet Heaven.

sentences.zh: 今冀已高讓，山陽君亦宜崇其本節。

translations.literal: Now Ji has already loftily declined; the Lady of Shanyang also should honor her original integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji has already demurred with lofty words; let Lady of Shanyang likewise cling to her modest virtue.

sentences.zh: 」雄言數切至，娥亦畏懼辭讓，而帝戀戀不能已，卒封之。

translations.literal: " Xiong's words were repeatedly cutting and direct; E also feared and declined with excuses, but the Emperor was lovingly unable to stop, and in the end enfeoffed her.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong's tone was blunt; Song E herself tried to decline; yet the emperor could not let go and enfeoffed her anyway.

sentences.zh: 後阿母遂以交遘失爵。

translations.literal: Afterward the wet-nurse thereupon because of mutual slander lost her noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: Later the nurse lost her title to factional slander.

sentences.zh: 是時大司農劉據以職事被譴，召詣尚書，傳呼促步，又加以捶撲。

translations.literal: At this time the Grand Minister of Agriculture Liu Ju, because of official matters, received reprimand, was summoned to the Masters of Writing, was urgently called in a hurried pace, and in addition was beaten with a club.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Minister of Agriculture Liu Ju was hauled before the Masters of Writing for a work fault, hustled in at a run, and flogged.

sentences.zh: 雄上言：「九卿位亞三事，班在大臣，行有佩玉之節，動有庠序之儀。

translations.literal: Xiong submitted words: "The nine ministers rank next to the Three Dukes; their class is among the great ministers; in walking they have the regulation of wearing jade pendants; in moving they have the ceremony of the schools."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong protested: "The nine ministers stand just below the Three Dukes; they are great officers whose pace should jingle with ritual jade and whose bearing should match the academy's dignity."

sentences.zh: [一]孝明皇帝始有撲罰，皆非古典。

translations.literal: [1] Emperor Xiaoming first had club beating as punishment; none of this is classical antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Clubbing ministers began under Emperor Ming—it is nowhere in the classical canon."

sentences.zh: 」帝從而改之，其後九卿無復捶撲者。

translations.literal: " The Emperor followed this and changed it; afterward the nine ministers never again had club beatings.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor accepted the reform, and no minister was beaten thereafter.

sentences.zh: 自雄掌納言，多所匡肅，每有章表奏議，台閣以為故事。

translations.literal: From the time Xiong held the intake of words, he in many places corrected and purified; whenever there were memorials and discussions in chapters and tables, the terrace and pavilion took them as precedent.
translations.idiomatic: Once Zuo Xiong controlled memorials, he cleaned up countless abuses; his memorials became models for the bureaus.

sentences.zh: 遷司隸校尉。

translations.literal: He was promoted Metropolitan Superintendent.
translations.idiomatic: He was raised to Metropolitan Superintendent.

sentences.zh: 注[一]禮記曰：「公侯佩山玄玉而朱組綬，大夫佩水蒼玉而緇組綬。」

translations.literal: Note [1] The Record of Rites says: "The duke and marquis wear dark mountain jade with vermilion group ribbons; the grandee wears water-green jade with black group ribbons."
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Book of Rites specifies, "Dukes and marquises wear dark mountain jade on vermilion cords; grandees wear water-azure jade on black cords."

sentences.zh: 初，雄薦周舉為尚書，舉既稱職，議者咸稱焉。

translations.literal: At first, Xiong recommended Zhou Ju to be Master of Writing; after Ju was praised as adequate to his post, debaters all praised him.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Zuo Xiong had recommended Zhou Ju for the Masters of Writing; Zhou proved equal to the job and won universal praise.

sentences.zh: 及在司隸，又舉故冀州刺史馮直以為將帥，而直嘗坐臧受罪，舉以此劾奏雄。

translations.literal: When he was Metropolitan Superintendent, he again recommended the former Governor of Ji, Feng Zhi, to be a general, but Zhi had once sat for corruption and received punishment; Ju on this account impeached Xiong in a memorial.
translations.idiomatic: As superintendent he nominated former Ji governor Feng Zhi for a military post—yet Feng had been convicted of graft; Zhou Ju impeached Zuo Xiong for it.

sentences.zh: 雄悅曰：「吾嘗事馮直之父而又與直善，今宣光以此奏吾，乃是韓厥之舉也。

translations.literal: Xiong joyfully said: "I once served Feng Zhi's father and moreover was on good terms with Zhi; now Xuanguang on this account memorializes against me—this is the recommendation of Han Jue."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong replied gladly: "I served Feng Zhi's father and was his friend; for Zhou Ju to impeach me over this is Han Jue's kind of integrity."

sentences.zh: 」由是天下服焉。

translations.literal: " Thereupon all under Heaven submitted.
translations.idiomatic: The empire admired him for it.

sentences.zh: [一]明年坐法免。

translations.literal: [1] The next year he was dismissed for violating the law.
translations.idiomatic: [1] The following year he lost his post for a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 後復為尚書。

translations.literal: Afterward he again became Master of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: He was later reappointed to the Masters of Writing.

sentences.zh: 永和三年卒。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yonghe he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Yonghe 3.

sentences.zh: 注[一]韓厥，韓獻子也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Han Jue was Han Xianzi.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Han Jue is the same man as Han Xianzi.

sentences.zh: 國語曰：「趙宣子舉獻子於靈公，以為司馬。

translations.literal: The Discourses of the States says: "Zhao Xuanzi recommended Xianzi to Duke Ling to be Minister of War."
translations.idiomatic: The Guoyu records that Zhao Dun recommended Han Jue to Duke Ling of Jin as minister of war.

sentences.zh: 河曲之役，宣子使人以其乘車干行，獻子執而戮之。

translations.literal: At the campaign of the River's Bend, Xuanzi sent a man to drive his carriage across the line of march; Xianzi seized him and executed him.
translations.idiomatic: During the battle at the Yellow River bend, Zhao Dun sent a driver to cut across the army's line; Han Jue arrested the man and had him executed.

sentences.zh: 宣子皆告諸大夫曰：『可賀我矣。

translations.literal: Xuanzi told all the grandees, saying: 'You may congratulate me.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Dun then told the ministers, 'Congratulate me.'

sentences.zh: 吾舉厥也而中吾，乃今知免於罪矣。』」

translations.literal: I recommended Jue and he hit my mark; only now do I know I am free from blame.'"
translations.idiomatic: 'I picked Han Jue, and he held me to the law—now I see I am cleared of fault.'"

sentences.zh: 周舉字宣光，汝南汝陽人，陳留太守防之子。

translations.literal: Zhou Ju, courtesy name Xuanguang, was a man of Ruyang in Runan, son of Grand Administrator Fang of Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju, courtesy Xuanguang, came from Ruyang in Runan and was the son of Chenliu governor Zhou Fang.

sentences.zh: 防在儒林傳。

translations.literal: Fang is in the biography of the Confucian Forest.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Fang has his own entry under the literati.

sentences.zh: 舉姿貌短陋，而博學洽聞，為儒者所宗，故京師為之語曰：「五經從橫周宣光。」

translations.literal: Ju was short and ugly in appearance, but broadly learned and well informed, and was taken as patriarch by Confucians; therefore the capital made a saying of him: "The Five Classics run crisscross—Zhou Xuanguang."
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju was plain and stunted in looks, yet so widely read that scholars took him as their authority—hence the capital rhyme: 'The Five Classics in every direction—Zhou Xuanguang.'

sentences.zh: 延*

translations.literal: Yan*
translations.idiomatic: In the Yanguang

translations.literal: (xi)
translations.idiomatic: (variant graph)

sentences.zh: **[光]*四年，辟司徒李合府。

translations.literal: **[guang]* fourth year, he was summoned to the office of Minister of Education Li He.
translations.idiomatic: In the fourth year of the Yanguang era he entered the bureau of Minister of Education Li He.

sentences.zh: 時宦者孫程等既立順帝，誅滅諸閻，議郎陳禪以為閻太后與帝無母子恩，宜徙別館，絕朝見。

translations.literal: At the time the eunuchs Sun Cheng and others had already established Emperor Shun, exterminated the various Yan; Gentleman Chen Chan thought that Empress Dowager Yan with the Emperor had no kindness of mother and son, and that she ought to be moved to a separate lodge and cut off from court audiences.
translations.idiomatic: After Sun Cheng's party had enthroned Emperor Shun and wiped out the Yan clan, Counselor Chen Chan argued that Empress Dowager Yan owed the emperor no maternal bond and should be packed off to a detached palace and denied audience.

sentences.zh: 腢臣議者鹹以為宜。

translations.literal: The ministers at discussion all thought it fitting.
translations.idiomatic: The court consensus favored Chen Chan's plan.

sentences.zh: 舉謂合曰：「昔鄭武姜謀殺嚴公，嚴公誓之黃泉；

translations.literal: Ju said to He: "In antiquity Lady Wu of Zheng plotted to kill Duke Zhuang; Duke Zhuang swore an oath at the Yellow Springs;
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju told Li He: "Long ago Lady Wu of Zheng schemed against Duke Zhuang, and the duke swore never to see her short of the underworld;"

sentences.zh: 秦始皇怨母失行，久而隔絕，後感穎考叔、茅焦之言，循復子道。

translations.literal: Qin Shihuang resented his mother's misconduct, and for a long time was cut off from her; afterward he was moved by the words of Ying Kaoshu of Ying and Mao Jiao, and restored the way of a son.
translations.idiomatic: the First Emperor of Qin loathed his mother's scandal and kept her at a distance until Ying Kaoshu and Mao Jiao moved him to resume a son's duty."

sentences.zh: 書傳美之。

translations.literal: Documents and transmissions praise this.
translations.idiomatic: Historians celebrate such reconciliations.

sentences.zh: [一]今諸閻新誅，太后幽在離宮，若悲愁生疾，一日不虞，主上將何以令於天下？

translations.literal: [1] Now the various Yan have newly been executed; the Empress Dowager is confined in a detached palace—if sorrow and grief produce illness, and one day the unexpected happens, how will the lord above command the realm under Heaven?
translations.idiomatic: [1] The Yan have just been slaughtered and the dowager languishes apart from court—if grief sickens her and she dies suddenly, how will Your Majesty answer the empire?"

sentences.zh: 如從禪議，後世歸咎明公。

translations.literal: If one follows Chan's proposal, later generations will lay the blame on the enlightened duke.
translations.idiomatic: Adopt Chen Chan's advice, and posterity will blame you, sir."

sentences.zh: 宜密表朝廷，令奉太后，率厲腢臣，朝覲如舊，以厭天心，以荅人望。

translations.literal: It is fitting secretly to memorialize the court, ordering that she be served as Empress Dowager, and that you lead all ministers in court audiences as of old, thereby to satisfy the mind of Heaven and answer the expectation of the people."
translations.idiomatic: Send a private memorial urging the throne to honor her as dowager, lead the ministers in the old audiences, satisfy Heaven, and meet the people's hopes."

sentences.zh: 」合即上疏陳之。

translations.literal: " He immediately submitted a memorial stating it.
translations.idiomatic: Li He did so at once.

sentences.zh: 明年正月，帝乃朝於東宮，太后由此以安。

translations.literal: In the first month of the next year, the Emperor thereupon held court at the Eastern Palace; the Empress Dowager thereby was secure.
translations.idiomatic: The next New Year the emperor appeared at the Eastern Palace, and the dowager's position was secured.

sentences.zh: 注[一]鄭武姜生莊公及共叔段，愛叔段，謀殺莊公。

translations.literal: Note [1] Lady Wu of Zheng bore Duke Zhuang and Lord Guanshu Duan; she loved Duan and plotted to kill Duke Zhuang.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: Lady Wu of Zheng favored her younger son Duan and plotted against Duke Zhuang.

sentences.zh: 公誓之曰：「不及黃泉，無相見也。

translations.literal: The duke swore to her, saying: "Unless we reach the Yellow Springs, we shall not see each other.
translations.idiomatic: The duke swore, 'We meet again only in the Yellow Springs.'

sentences.zh: 」既而悔之。

translations.literal: " Later he regretted it.
translations.idiomatic: He soon repented.

sentences.zh: 穎考叔為穎谷封人，曰：「若掘地及泉，隧而相見，其誰曰不然！

translations.literal: Ying Kaoshu was border officer of Ying valley, saying: "If you dig until you reach spring water and meet through a tunnel, who will say it is not so!"
translations.idiomatic: Ying Kaoshu of Ying Valley said, 'Dig until you strike water and meet through a tunnel—who will call that breaking your oath?'

sentences.zh: 」公從之，遂為母子如初。

translations.literal: " The duke followed this, and thereupon became mother and son as at first.
translations.idiomatic: The duke agreed, and mother and son were reconciled.

sentences.zh: 事見左傳。

translations.literal: The affair is seen in the Zuo Tradition.
translations.idiomatic: The story is in the Zuo zhuan.

sentences.zh: 茅焦事，解見蘇竟傳也。

translations.literal: The affair of Mao Jiao is explained in the biography of Su Jing.
translations.idiomatic: Mao Jiao's story is told in the biography of Su Jing.

sentences.zh: 後長樂少府朱倀[一]代合為司徒，舉猶為吏。

translations.literal: Afterward Intendant of the Palace of Eternal Joy Zhu Chang [1] replaced He as Minister of Education; Ju still served as clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Later Chief Intendant Zhu Chang succeeded Li He as minister while Zhou Ju remained on his staff.

sentences.zh: 時孫程等坐懷表上殿爭功，帝怒，悉徙封遠縣，□洛陽令促期發遣。

translations.literal: At the time Sun Cheng and others sat guilty of wearing memorial plaques to the upper hall to dispute merit; the Emperor was angry and moved all their enfeoffments to distant counties, [ordering] the magistrate of Luoyang to hurry the deadline for dispatch.
translations.idiomatic: When Sun Cheng's faction wore tablets into the hall to wrangle over rewards, the emperor banished them to distant fiefs and ordered the Luoyang magistrate to hustle them out of the capital.

sentences.zh: 舉說朱倀曰：「朝廷在西鐘下時，非孫程等豈立？

translations.literal: Ju persuaded Zhu Chang, saying: "When the court was beneath the west bell, without Sun Cheng and the others could it have been established?
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju urged Zhu Chang: 'When the court cowered under the west-bell coup, without Sun Cheng's band who would be emperor?

sentences.zh: [二]雖韓、彭、吳、賈之功，何以加諸！

translations.literal: [2] Though the achievements of Han, Peng, Wu, and Jia—how could one add to them!"
translations.idiomatic: [2] Not even Han Xin, Peng Yue, Wu Han, or Jia Fu did more.'

sentences.zh: [三]今忘其大德，錄其小過，如道路夭折，帝有殺功臣之譏。

translations.literal: [3] Now forgetting their great virtue and recording their small faults—if on the road they meet untimely death, the Emperor will have the reputation of killing meritorious ministers.
translations.idiomatic: [3] To forget that debt and harp on petty faults—if they die on the road, the throne will wear the stain of executing its benefactors.'

sentences.zh: 及今未去，宜急表之。

translations.literal: Before they have not yet departed, it is fitting urgently to memorialize them.
translations.idiomatic: Memorialize now, before they are gone.'

sentences.zh: 」倀曰：「今詔怒，二尚書已奏其事，吾獨表此，必致罪譴。

translations.literal: " Zhu said: "Now the edict is angry; the two Masters of Writing have already memorialized the affair; if I alone memorialize this, I will surely incur blame and reprimand."
translations.idiomatic: Zhu answered: 'The emperor is furious and both Masters of Writing have already endorsed the exile—if I intervene alone I invite punishment.'

sentences.zh: 」舉曰：「明公年過八十，位為台輔，不於今時竭忠報國，惜身安寵，欲以何求？

translations.literal: " Ju said: "The enlightened duke has passed eighty years; his position is that of terrace pillar—if not at this time you exhaust loyalty in repaying the state, but spare your person and cherish favor, what do you seek?
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju replied: 'You are past eighty and sit as one of the three pillars—if you will not spend your credit for the state now, hoarding safety and favor, what is left to want?'

sentences.zh: 祿位雖全，必陷佞邪之譏；

translations.literal: Though salary and rank are complete, you will surely fall into the slander of flattery and evil;
translations.idiomatic: You may keep rank and salary, but you will be remembered as a time-serving flatterer;'

sentences.zh: 諫而獲罪，猶有忠貞之名。

translations.literal: if you remonstrate and meet punishment, you still will have the name of loyalty and integrity."
translations.idiomatic: 'speak out and suffer—yet you win a loyalist's name.'

sentences.zh: 若舉言不足采，請從此辭。

translations.literal: If my words are not worth gathering, please from this dismiss me."
translations.idiomatic: If my counsel is worthless, dismiss me from your service.'

sentences.zh: 」倀乃表諫，帝果從之。

translations.literal: " Zhu thereupon memorialized in remonstrance, and the Emperor indeed followed it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Chang memorialized; the emperor relented.

sentences.zh: 注[一]音丑良反。

translations.literal: Note [1] It is read chou liang fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the name is read chou liang fan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]朝廷謂順帝也。

translations.literal: Note [2] "Court" means Emperor Shun.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: 'the court' refers to Emperor Shun's party.

sentences.zh: 孫程與王康等十八人謀於西鐘下，共立濟陰王為順帝也。

translations.literal: Sun Cheng with Wang Kang and others, eighteen men, plotted beneath the west bell, jointly establishing the Prince of Jiyin as Emperor Shun.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Cheng, Wang Kang, and sixteen others plotted at the west bell to enthrone the Prince of Jiyin as Emperor Shun.

sentences.zh: 注[三]韓信、彭越、吳漢、賈復也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Han Xin, Peng Yue, Wu Han, and Jia Fu.
translations.idiomatic: Note [3]: Han Xin, Peng Yue, Wu Han, and Jia Fu.

sentences.zh: 舉後舉茂才，為平丘令。

translations.literal: Later Ju was recommended as outstanding talent and became magistrate of Pingqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Later, nominated as maocai, he became magistrate of Pingqiu.

sentences.zh: [一]上書言當世得失，辭甚切正。

translations.literal: [1] He submitted a memorial on the gains and losses of the age; the wording was very cutting and correct.
translations.idiomatic: [1] He memorialized on current policy in language that was blunt and sound.

sentences.zh: 尚書郭虔、應賀等見之歎息，共上疏稱舉忠直，欲帝置章御坐，以為規誡。

translations.literal: Masters of Writing Guo Qian, Ying He, and others saw it and sighed; they jointly submitted a memorial praising Ju as loyal and upright, wishing the Emperor to place the document on the imperial seat to serve as regulation and warning.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Qian, Ying He, and their colleagues read it with admiration and jointly urged the throne to keep the memorial on the imperial desk as a standing reminder.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]平丘，縣，屬陳留郡。

translations.literal: [2] Note [1] Pingqiu is a county belonging to Chenliu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Note [1]: Pingqiu county lay in Chenliu commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[二]章謂所上之書。

translations.literal: Note [2] Zhang means the document that was submitted.
translations.idiomatic: Note [2]: 'zhang' means the memorial itself.

sentences.zh: 舉稍遷并州刺史。

translations.literal: Ju was gradually promoted to Governor of Bing Province.
translations.idiomatic: He rose step by step to governor of Bing Province.

sentences.zh: 太原一郡，舊俗以介子推焚骸，有龍忌之禁。

translations.literal: In Taiyuan commandery, the old custom, because of Jie Zitui's burned bones, had a taboo of the dragon ji against fire.
translations.idiomatic: Taiyuan still observed a spring taboo on fire for fear of offending the spirit of Jie Zitui, whom legend said Duke Wen had burned on the hill.

sentences.zh: [一]至其亡月，鹹言神靈不樂舉火，由是士民每冬中輒一月寒食，莫敢煙爨，老小不堪，歲多死者。

translations.literal: [1] When it reached the month of his death, all said the spirits did not delight in raising fire; whereby gentry and commoners every mid-winter then for one month ate cold food, none daring to smoke or cook; old and young could not endure it, and many died each year.
translations.idiomatic: [1] In the month of his death people said the gods forbade fires, so for a midwinter month none would light a stove; the old and weak perished by the score.

sentences.zh: 舉既到州，乃作弔書以置子推之廟，言盛冬去火，殘損民命，非賢者之意，以宣示愚民，使還溫食。

translations.literal: When Ju arrived at the province, he then composed a condolence document and placed it in Zitui's temple, saying that in deep winter to remove fire injures the people's lives and is not the intent of a worthy man; he displayed it to instruct the foolish people, causing them to return to warm food.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju posted a tract at Jie Zitui's shrine arguing that midwinter bans on fire wasted lives and shamed a worthy man's memory, and urged the people back to cooked food.

sentences.zh: [二]於是觿惑稍解，風俗頗革。

translations.literal: [2] Thereupon the multitude's delusion gradually loosened, and the custom was somewhat reformed.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Superstition eased and the harmful custom faded.

sentences.zh: 注[一]新序曰：「晉文公反國，介子推無爵，遂去而之介山之上。

translations.literal: Note [1] The New Prefaces says: "When Duke Wen of Jin returned to his state, Jie Zitui had no rank, and thereupon departed and went to Mount Jie.
translations.idiomatic: Note [1]: the Xin xu says that when Duke Wen of Jin took power, Jie Zitui, having no fief, withdrew to Mount Jie.

sentences.zh: 文公求之不得，乃焚其山，推遂不出而焚死。

translations.literal: Wen sought him and could not obtain him, and thereupon burned the mountain; Zitui thereupon did not come out and burned to death.
