translations.idiomatic: Even worthy men Liang Ji had sponsored received no appointment from Huang Qiong.

sentences.zh: 延熹元年，以日食免。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yanxi, because of solar eclipse he was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Yanxi 1: he stepped down after a solar eclipse.

sentences.zh: 復為大司農。

translations.literal: He again became Grand Minister of Agriculture.
translations.idiomatic: He returned as grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 明年，梁冀被誅，太尉胡廣、司徒韓演、司空孫朗皆坐阿附免廢，復拜瓊為太尉。

translations.literal: The next year, when Liang Ji was executed, Grand Commandant Hu Guang, Minister of Education Han Yan, and Minister of Works Sun Lang all sat for fawning attachment and were dismissed; Qiong was again appointed Grand Commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The next year Liang Ji fell; Hu Guang, Han Yan, and Sun Lang lost their posts for toadying, and Huang Qiong was again made grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 以師傅之恩，而不阿梁氏，乃封為邟鄉侯，[一]邑千戶。

translations.literal: Because of the kindness of tutor and protector, yet he did not fawn on the Liang clan, he was thereupon enfeoffed as marquis of Xiang village, [1] with a fief of one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: For tutoring the emperor without cringing to Liang Ji he was enfeoffed as marquis of Xiang township with a thousand households.'

sentences.zh: 瓊辭疾讓封六七上，言旨懇惻，乃許之。

translations.literal: Qiong declined illness and yielded the enfeoffment six or seven times; his words were earnest and sincere; thereupon permission was granted.
translations.idiomatic: He pleaded illness and returned the fief six or seven times until the throne relented.

sentences.zh: 梁冀既誅，瓊首居公位，舉奏州郡素行貪污至死徙者十餘人，海內由是翕然望之。

translations.literal: After Liang Ji was executed, Qiong first occupied the highest office; he impeached more than ten governors and commandery ministers whose habitual conduct had been greedy and corrupt down to execution or banishment; within the seas thereupon uniformly looked up to him.
translations.idiomatic: First among the three dukes after Liang Ji's death, he impeached a dozen venal governors to death or exile, and the empire looked to him.

sentences.zh: 尋而五侯□權，傾動內外，自度力不能匡，乃稱疾不起。

translations.literal: Soon the five marquises monopolized power and overturned within and without; he measured himself as unable to correct it, and thereupon claimed illness and did not rise.
translations.idiomatic: When the five marquises seized power he knew he could not check them and stayed home "ill."

sentences.zh: [二]四年，以寇賊免。

translations.literal: [2] In the fourth year, because of bandits he was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: [2] In the fourth year banditry cost him his post.'

sentences.zh: 其年復為司空。

translations.literal: That year he again became Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: The same year he was minister of works again.

sentences.zh: 秋，以地震免。

translations.literal: In autumn, because of earthquake he was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: That autumn an earthquake removed him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]說文云「邟，穎川縣」也。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Shuowen says "Xiang is a county in Yingchuan."
translations.idiomatic: Note one: the Shuowen places Xiang in Yingchuan.'

sentences.zh: 漢穎川有周承休侯國，元始二年更名曰邟，音亢。

translations.literal: Han Yingchuan had the marquisate of Zhou Chengxiu; in the second year of Yuan beginning it was renamed Xiang, read kang.
translations.idiomatic: Han's Zhou Chengxiu marquisate was renamed Xiang in Yuan 2, pronounced kang.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]五侯謂左悺、徐璜等。

translations.literal: Note [2] The five marquises mean Zuo Guan, Xu Huang, and others.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: the five marquises are Zuo Guan, Xu Huang, and their clique.'

sentences.zh: 七年，疾篤，上疏諫曰：「臣聞天者務剛其氣，君者務強其政。

translations.literal: In the seventh year, gravely ill, he submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying: "Your subject has heard that Heaven is bent on hardening its qi, and the ruler is bent on strengthening his government.
translations.idiomatic: In year seven, dying, he wrote: 'Heaven insists on firm breath; the ruler must stiffen his rule.'

sentences.zh: 是以王者處高自持，不可不安；

translations.literal: Therefore the king occupies height and holds himself—he cannot but be secure;
translations.idiomatic: A king on the height must stay balanced;'

sentences.zh: 履危任力，不可不據。

translations.literal: treads peril and trusts to strength—he cannot but have a foothold.
translations.idiomatic: on a cliff he must plant his feet.'

sentences.zh: 夫自持不安則顛，任力不據則危。

translations.literal: As for holding oneself without security, then one topples; trusting to strength without a foothold, then there is danger.
translations.idiomatic: Lose your balance and you fall; lose your footing and you plunge.'

sentences.zh: 故聖人升高據上，則以德義為首；

translations.literal: Therefore the sage ascends height and occupies the top, then takes virtue and righteousness as head;
translations.idiomatic: So the sage on the height puts virtue first;'

sentences.zh: 涉危蹈傾，則以賢者為力。

translations.literal: enters peril and treads overturning, then takes the worthy as strength.
translations.idiomatic: in danger he leans on the worthy.'

sentences.zh: 唐堯以德化為冠冕，以稷、契為筋力。

translations.literal: Tang Yao took virtue-transformation as cap and crown, and Yi and Xie as sinew and strength.
translations.idiomatic: Yao wore moral sway as his crown and used Yi and Xie as sinews.'

sentences.zh: 高而益崇，動而愈據，此先聖所以長守萬國，保其社稷者也。

translations.literal: High yet more lofty, moving yet more secure—this is how former sages long guarded the myriad states and protected their altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: Thus ancient kings grew higher yet steadier and long kept their altars.'

sentences.zh: 言之者必族，附之者必榮。

translations.literal: Those who speak of it meet clan execution; those who attach to it meet glory.
translations.idiomatic: Truth-tellers face extermination; toadies flourish.'

sentences.zh: 忠臣懼死而杜口，萬夫怖禍而木舌，[四]塞陛下耳目之明，更為聾瞽之主。

translations.literal: Loyal ministers fear death and shut their mouths; the myriad commoners fear disaster and are wooden-tongued, [4] blocking Your Majesty's eyes and ears's brightness, making you instead a deaf and blind ruler.
translations.idiomatic: Loyal men seal their lips for fear of death, the people hold their tongues for fear of harm, and your eyes and ears go dark.'

sentences.zh: 故太尉李固、杜喬，忠以直言，德以輔政，念國亡身，隕歿為報，而坐陳國議，遂見殘滅。

translations.literal: Therefore Grand Commandants Li Gu and Du Qiao, loyal in straight words, virtuous in assisting government, thought on the state and forgot their persons, dying in ruin as repayment—yet because they sat in Chen's factional counsel, thereupon they met mutilation and extinction.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu and Du Qiao gave straight counsel for the state and died for it—yet Chen's faction destroyed them.'

sentences.zh: [五]賢愚切痛，海內傷懼。

translations.literal: [5] Worthy and foolish alike cut to the heart; within the seas they were wounded and afraid.
translations.idiomatic: [5] Worthy and base alike were shaken with fear.'

sentences.zh: 又前白馬令李雲，指言宦官罪穢宜誅，皆因觿人之心，以救積薪之敝。

translations.literal: Also previously Prefect of Baima Li Yun, pointing out that the eunuchs' crimes and filth ought to be executed, all followed the multitude's mind to save the piled-brambles ill.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Baima prefect Li Yun demanded eunuchs be executed for their crimes—speaking for every honest heart.'

sentences.zh: [六]弘農杜觿，知雲所言宜行，懼雲以忠獲罪，故上書陳理之，乞同日而死，所以感悟國家，庶雲獲免。

translations.literal: [6] Du Zhong of Hongnong knew Yun's words ought to be enacted; fearing Yun would for loyalty meet punishment, therefore submitted a memorial stating his case, begging to die the same day, thereby to move the state and perhaps let Yun escape.
translations.idiomatic: [6] Du Zhong of Hongnong knew Li Yun was right and offered to die with him to move the court.'

sentences.zh: 而雲既不辜，觿又並坐，天下尤痛，益以怨結，故朝野之人，以忠為諱。

translations.literal: Yet Yun was not guilty, and Zhong also sat together; all under Heaven especially grieved, and resentment increased the more; therefore those at court and in the wild took loyalty as a taboo.
translations.idiomatic: Both were punished; the empire grieved and learned to shun loyalty.'

sentences.zh: 昔趙殺鳴犢，孔子臨河而反。

translations.literal: In antiquity Zhao killed Mingdu; Confucius reached the river and turned back.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao killed the worthy Mingdu, Confucius turned back from the Yellow River.'

sentences.zh: 夫覆巢破卵，則鳳皇不翔；

translations.literal: When nests are overturned and eggs broken, then the phoenix does not soar;
translations.idiomatic: Overturn the nest and the phoenix will not circle;'

sentences.zh: 刳牲夭胎，則麒麟不臻。

translations.literal: when pregnant beasts are disemboweled and fetal young killed, then the unicorn does not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: gut a pregnant doe and the unicorn stays away.'

sentences.zh: 誠物類相感，理使其然。

translations.literal: Truly things of a kind respond to each other; principle makes it so.
translations.idiomatic: Like calls to like—Heaven's logic.'

sentences.zh: [七]尚書周永，昔為沛令，素事梁冀，幸其威埶，坐事當罪，越拜令職。

translations.literal: [7] Master of Writing Zhou Yong, formerly when he was magistrate of Pei, ordinarily served Liang Ji, trusted in his power and situation, sat in an affair deserving punishment, leaped to appointment as magistrate.
translations.idiomatic: [7] Zhou Yong of the Masters of Writing once governed Pei under Liang Ji, dodged a crime, and vaulted to office.'

sentences.zh: 見冀將衰，乃陽毀示忠，遂因奸計，亦取封侯。

translations.literal: Seeing Ji was about to decline, then feigned destruction to show loyalty; thereupon by crafty plan also obtained enfeoffment as marquis.
translations.idiomatic: When Ji fell he pretended to have turned on him and won a marquisate by intrigue.'

sentences.zh: 又黃門協邪，腢輩相黨，自冀興盛，腹背相親，朝夕圖謀，共構奸軌。

translations.literal: Also the Yellow Gate assists evil; the multitude forms factions; from the time Ji flourished, belly and back were mutually intimate; morning and evening they plotted together, jointly constructing wicked tracks.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs and Liang Ji were belly-to-back conspirators for years.'

sentences.zh: 臨冀當誅，無可設巧，復記其惡，以要爵賞。

translations.literal: Facing Ji's moment of execution, having no way to set up craftiness, again recorded his evils in order to seek noble reward.
translations.idiomatic: At Ji's execution they had no tricks left but to list his crimes and claim reward.'

sentences.zh: 陛下不加清澄，審別真偽，復與忠臣並時顯封，使朱紫共色，粉墨雜蹂，所謂扺金玉於沙礫，[八]碎珪璧於泥塗。

translations.literal: Your Majesty does not add clear purification, carefully distinguishing true and false, again together with loyal ministers at the same time conspicuously enfeoffs them, causing crimson and purple to share color, powder and ink to be mixed and trampled—what is called pressing gold and jade into sand and gravel, [8] smashing jade tablets and disks into mud and mire.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you ennobled them with true ministers—vermilion and violet in one dye, jewels cast in gravel.'

sentences.zh: 四方聞之，莫不憤歎。

translations.literal: The four quarters hearing it, none did not sigh in anger.
translations.idiomatic: The four quarters raged at the sight.

sentences.zh: 昔曾子大孝，慈母投杼；

translations.literal: In antiquity Zengzi was greatly filial; his kind mother cast aside her shuttle;
translations.idiomatic: Zengzi's mother once threw her shuttle, fooled by slander;'

sentences.zh: [九]伯奇至賢，終於流放。

translations.literal: [9] Boqi was supremely worthy; in the end he was banished.
translations.idiomatic: [9] Boqi was supremely worthy yet banished.'

sentences.zh: [一０]夫讒諛所舉，無高而不可升；

translations.literal: [10] What slander and flattery raise, none is so high it cannot be climbed;
translations.idiomatic: [10] Flattery lifts the vile to any height;'

sentences.zh: [阿黨]相抑，無深而不可淪。

translations.literal: Fawning factions mutually suppress—none is so deep it cannot be sunk.
translations.idiomatic: When cliques pull down the worthy, none sinks deeper than the good man.'

sentences.zh: 可不察歟？

translations.literal: Can one not examine?
translations.idiomatic: Will you not look closely?'

sentences.zh: 臣至頑駑，世荷國恩，身輕位重，勤不補過，然懼於永歿，負釁益深。

translations.literal: Your subject is extremely stupid and slow, generation after generation receiving the state's grace; his person is light while his position is heavy; diligence does not mend his faults—yet he fears that at final death his burden of guilt will deepen the more.
translations.idiomatic: I am a dull man long favored by the throne; my body is slight but my office heavy; diligence cannot mend my faults, yet I dread dying with a heavier guilt.'

sentences.zh: 敢以垂絕之日，陳不諱之言，庶有萬分，無恨三泉。

translations.literal: I dare on the day of hanging thread to state words without taboo, hoping perhaps in one ten-thousandth, to be without regret toward the three springs.
translations.idiomatic: On death's eve I speak without reserve, hoping for one chance in ten thousand to rest easy below.'

sentences.zh: 」[一一]其年卒，時年七十九。

translations.literal: "[11] That year he died, aged seventy-nine sui.
translations.idiomatic: [11] He died that year at seventy-nine.'

sentences.zh: 贈車騎將軍，謚曰忠侯。

translations.literal: He was posthumously awarded General of Chariots and Cavalry; his posthumous title was Loyal Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: The court titled him general of chariots and cavalry and posthumously "Loyal Marquis."'

sentences.zh: 孫琬。

translations.literal: His grandson was Wan.
translations.idiomatic: His grandson was Huang Wan.

sentences.zh: 注[一]泮冰諭危陷。

translations.literal: Note [1] Floating ice illustrates peril and sinking.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: floating ice images danger.'

sentences.zh: 枳棘諭艱難。

translations.literal: Thorns and brambles illustrate hardship.
translations.idiomatic: Thorns image hardship.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]形，兆也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Xing means omen.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: xing means portent.'

sentences.zh: 言未有天下之兆。

translations.literal: It means there was not yet an omen of possessing the realm under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: There was as yet no sign he would own the realm.'

sentences.zh: 「畫」或作「書」也。

translations.literal: "Paint" sometimes is written "document."
translations.idiomatic: Some texts write "paint" as "write."'

sentences.zh: 注[三]殷，盛也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Yin means flourishing.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: yin means flourishing.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]法言曰「金口木舌」也。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Fayan says "metal mouth and wooden tongue."
translations.idiomatic: Note four: Yang Xiong's Fayan speaks of the wooden-tongued bell.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]坐音才臥反。

translations.literal: Note [5] Zuo is read cai wo fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note five: zuo is read cai wo fan.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]賈誼上疏曰「夫抱火厝之積薪之下而寢其上，火未及然，因謂之安。

translations.literal: Note [6] Jia Yi submitted a memorial saying "To embrace fire and set it beneath piled kindling and sleep on top—before the fire catches, therefore one calls it safe.
translations.idiomatic: Note six: Jia Yi likened the state to sleeping on kindling before the fire catches.'

sentences.zh: 方今之政，何以異此」也。

translations.literal: How does today's government differ from this?"
translations.idiomatic: How is today's court any different?'

sentences.zh: 注[七]史記曰，孔子將西見趙簡子，至於河而聞竇鳴犢、舜華之死也，臨河而歎曰：「美哉洋洋，丘之不濟此，命也夫！

translations.literal: Note [7] The Records of the Historian says that when Confucius was about to go west to see Zhao Jianzi, reaching the river he heard of the deaths of Dou Mingdu and Shunhua, and facing the river sighed, saying: "How beautiful, vast and flowing—I will not cross this; is it fate!
translations.idiomatic: Note seven: the Shiji says Confucius turned back from the Yellow River on hearing Dou Mingdu and Shunhua had been killed.'

sentences.zh: 竇鳴犢、舜華，晉之賢大夫也。

translations.literal: Dou Mingdu and Shunhua were Jin's worthy grandees.
translations.idiomatic: They were worthy grandees of Jin.'

sentences.zh: 趙簡子未得志之時，須此兩人而後從政，及其得志而殺之。

translations.literal: When Zhao Jianzi had not yet obtained his will, he needed these two men and afterward handled government; when he obtained his will, he killed them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Jianzi needed them before he rose, then slew them once he had power.'

sentences.zh: 丘聞刳胎殺夭，則麒麟不至郊藪；

translations.literal: I have heard that when you disembowel the pregnant and kill the unborn, then the unicorn does not reach the suburban marsh;
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, 'Slaughter the carrying dam and the unicorn shuns the royal park;'

sentences.zh: 涸澤而漁，則蛟龍不合陰陽；

translations.literal: when you drain the marsh and fish, then the horned dragon does not join yin and yang;
translations.idiomatic: drain the pool for fish and the dragon will not harmonize yin and yang.'

sentences.zh: 覆巢毀卵，則鳳皇不翔。

translations.literal: When nests are overturned and eggs destroyed, then the phoenix does not soar.
translations.idiomatic: Smash the nest and the phoenix will not circle.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why?
translations.idiomatic: Why is that?

sentences.zh: 君子諱傷其類也。

translations.literal: The nobleman shrinks from wounding his kind.
translations.idiomatic: A gentleman hates to harm his own kind.

sentences.zh: 」事亦見孔子家語文也。

translations.literal: " The affair is also seen in the text of the Kongzi jiayu.
translations.idiomatic: The same story appears in the Family Sayings of Confucius.

sentences.zh: 注[八]扺，投也。

translations.literal: Note [8] Di means to cast or throw.
translations.idiomatic: Note eight: di means to cast aside.

sentences.zh: 音紙。

translations.literal: It is read zhi.
translations.idiomatic: The reading is zhi.

sentences.zh: 注[九]解見寇榮傳。

translations.literal: Note [9] The explanation is seen in the biography of Kou Rong.
translations.idiomatic: Note nine: see Kou Rong's biography.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]說苑曰「王國子前母子伯奇，後母子伯封。

translations.literal: Note [10] The Shuoyuan says: "The king of Guo had a former wife's son Boqi and a later wife's son Bofeng.
translations.idiomatic: Note ten: the Shuoyuan tells how the king of Guo had Boqi by a first wife and Bofeng by a second.

sentences.zh: 後母欲其子立為太子，說王曰：『伯奇好妾。

translations.literal: The stepmother wished her son to be established as heir apparent, and persuaded the king, saying: 'Boqi favors concubines.'
translations.idiomatic: The stepmother wanted her son heir and told the king Boqi chased women.

sentences.zh: 』王不信。

translations.literal: " The king did not believe.
translations.idiomatic: The king did not believe her.

sentences.zh: 其母曰：『令伯奇於後園，妾過其旁，王上台視之，即可知。

translations.literal: His mother said: 'Order Boqi to the rear garden; let the concubine pass beside him; the king ascends the terrace and watches—then you will know.'
translations.idiomatic: She said, 'Send Boqi to the back garden; I will walk past him; watch from the terrace.'

sentences.zh: 』王如其言，伯奇入園，後母陰取蜂十數置單衣中，過伯奇邊曰：『蜂螫我。

translations.literal: " The king did as her words; Boqi entered the garden; the stepmother secretly took a dozen bees and placed them in a single-layer robe, passed Boqi's side and said: 'Bees sting me.'
translations.idiomatic: The king agreed. Boqi entered the garden; she hid bees in a robe, walked past him, and cried that bees were stinging her.

sentences.zh: 』伯奇就衣中取蜂殺之。

translations.literal: " Boqi approached the robe, took the bees, and killed them.
translations.idiomatic: Boqi reached into the robe, took the bees, and crushed them.

sentences.zh: 王遙見之，乃逐伯奇」也。

translations.literal: The king from afar saw it, and thereupon drove Boqi away."
translations.idiomatic: From a distance the king thought Boqi molested her and banished him.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]三者數之極。

translations.literal: Note [11] Three is the extreme of numbers.
translations.idiomatic: Note eleven: three is the extreme number.

sentences.zh: 一生二，二生三，三生萬物，天地人之極數。

translations.literal: One produces two, two produces three, three produces the myriad things—the extreme number of Heaven, earth, and man.
translations.idiomatic: One begets two, two begets three, three begets the myriad things—the cosmic triad.

sentences.zh: 故以三為名者，取其深之極也。

translations.literal: Therefore those who take three as a name take its meaning of extreme depth.
translations.idiomatic: Hence 'three' in titles marks the utmost depth.

sentences.zh: 孫 黃琬

translations.literal: Grandson Huang Wan
translations.idiomatic: His grandson: Huang Wan

sentences.zh: 琬字子琰。

translations.literal: Wan's courtesy name was Ziyan.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Wan, courtesy Ziyan,

sentences.zh: 少失父。

translations.literal: In youth he lost his father.
translations.idiomatic: lost his father while young.

sentences.zh: 早而辯慧。

translations.literal: Early he was eloquent and clever.
translations.idiomatic: He was precociously clever.

sentences.zh: 祖父瓊，初為魏郡太守，建和元年正月日食，京師不見而瓊以狀聞。

translations.literal: His grandfather Qiong, when he first was Grand Administrator of Wei commandery, in the first month of the first year of Jianhe there was a solar eclipse; the capital did not see it, yet Qiong reported the circumstance.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Huang Qiong, as Wei governor in Jianhe 1, reported a solar eclipse invisible in the capital.

sentences.zh: 太后詔問所食多少，瓊思其對而未知所況。

translations.literal: The Empress Dowager by edict asked how much had been eaten; Qiong pondered his reply but did not know how to describe it.
translations.idiomatic: The dowager asked how much of the sun was eclipsed; Huang Qiong could not find words.

sentences.zh: 琬年七歲，在傍，曰：「何不言日食之餘，如月之初？

translations.literal: Wan, aged seven, at his side, said: "Why not say that the remainder of the eclipse is like the moon at its first crescent?
translations.idiomatic: Seven-year-old Huang Wan said, 'Say the uneclipsed sliver looks like a new moon.'

sentences.zh: 」瓊大驚，即以其言應詔，而深奇愛之。

translations.literal: " Qiong was greatly startled and immediately used his words to answer the edict, and deeply marveled at and loved him.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong was astonished, used the line in his memorial, and cherished the boy.

sentences.zh: 後瓊為司徒，琬以公孫拜童子郎，辭病不就，知名京師。

translations.literal: Afterward when Qiong was Minister of Education, Wan because of grandson's status was appointed boy Gentleman; he declined illness and did not take office, but was famous in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: When Huang Qiong became minister of education, Wan was offered child Gentleman by kinship; he pleaded illness but was already famous at court.

sentences.zh: 時司空盛允有疾，瓊遣琬候問，會江夏上蠻賊事副府，[一]允發書視畢，微戲琬曰：「江夏大邦，而蠻多士少。

translations.literal: At the time Minister of Works Sheng Yun was ill; Qiong sent Wan to inquire after him; it happened that Jiangxia submitted a duplicate copy of a memorial on Man banditry to the office, [1] when Yun opened the document and finished reading, he slightly teased Wan, saying: "Jiangxia is a great state, yet the Man are many and the scholars few.
translations.idiomatic: Sheng Yun, ill, was visited by Wan. A Jiangxia report on Man rebels lay on the desk. Yun teased him: 'Great Jiangxia—many barbarians, few scholars.'

sentences.zh: 」琬奉手對曰：「蠻夷猾夏，責在司空。

translations.literal: " Wan folded his hands and replied: "The Man and Yi trouble the Xia—responsibility lies with the Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: Wan bowed and said, 'Barbarians vex the heartland—the Minister of Works is to blame.'

sentences.zh: 」因拂衣辭去。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he brushed his robe and took leave.
translations.idiomatic: He swept his sleeves and left.

sentences.zh: 允甚奇之。

translations.literal: Yun greatly marveled at him.
translations.idiomatic: Sheng Yun admired him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]副本詣公府也。

translations.literal: Note [1] The duplicate copy goes to the public office.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: the duplicate went to the ministry.

sentences.zh: 稍遷五官中郎將。

translations.literal: He was gradually promoted General of the Household for All Purposes.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to general of the household for all purposes.

sentences.zh: 時陳蕃為光祿勳，深相敬待，數與議事。

translations.literal: At the time Chen Fan was Director of the Masters of Splendid Carriages; he deeply respected and treated him well, repeatedly discussing affairs with him.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Fan, then director of splendid carriages, respected him and often debated policy with him.

sentences.zh: 舊制，光祿舉三署郎，以高功久次才德尤異者為茂才四行。

translations.literal: By old regulation the Splendid Carriages recommended gentlemen of the three bureaus, taking those of high merit, long seniority, and outstanding talent and virtue as maocai in the four conduct grades.
translations.idiomatic: By rule the director nominated gentlemen of the three corps for maocai on four moral criteria.

sentences.zh: [一]時權富子弟多以人事得舉，而貧約守志者以窮退見遺，京師為之謠曰：「欲得不能，光祿茂才。

translations.literal: [1] At the time sons of the powerful mostly obtained recommendation through connections, while the poor who kept their integrity were cast aside for poverty—the capital made a rhyme of it: "Want it—can't get it; Splendid Carriages, maocai.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Rich youths bought nominations while poor scholars were passed over—hence the rhyme, 'Want it, can't have it—Splendid Carriages' maocai.'

sentences.zh: 」[二]於是琬、蕃同心，顯用志士，平原劉醇、河東朱山、蜀郡殷參等並以才行蒙舉。

translations.literal: "[2] Thereupon Wan and Fan with one mind openly employed men of purpose—Liu Chun of Pingyuan, Zhu Shan of Hedong, Yin Shen of Shujun, and others all by conduct and ability received recommendation.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Wan and Chen Fan then promoted Liu Chun, Zhu Shan, Yin Shen, and others on merit.

sentences.zh: 蕃、琬遂為權富郎所見中傷，事下御史[中]丞王暢、侍御史刁韙。

translations.literal: Fan and Wan thereupon were slandered by the gentlemen of the powerful; the affair went down to Imperial Clerk [Central] Director Wang Chang and attendant censor Diao Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Powerful gentlemen slandered them; the case went to Wang Chang and Diao Wei.

sentences.zh: 韙、暢素重蕃、琬，不舉其事，而左右復陷以朋黨。

translations.literal: Wei and Chang always esteemed Fan and Wan and did not impeach the affair, but those to the left and right again trapped them with faction.
translations.idiomatic: Chang and Wei respected them and filed no charge, yet palace factions smeared them as a clique.

sentences.zh: 暢坐左轉議郎而免蕃官，琬、韙俱禁錮。

translations.literal: Chang sat for demotion to Gentleman Consultant and Fan was dismissed from office; Wan and Wei were both barred from office.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Chang was demoted, Chen Fan dismissed, Huang Wan and Diao Wei both proscribed.

sentences.zh: 注[一]久次謂久居官次也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Long seniority means long occupying official seniority.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: 'long seniority' means long time in rank.

sentences.zh: 注[二]能音乃來反。

translations.literal: Note [2] Neng is read nai lai fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: neng is read nai lai fan.

sentences.zh: 韙字子榮，彭城人。

translations.literal: Wei's courtesy name was Zirong; he was a man of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Diao Wei, courtesy Zirong, was from Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 後陳蕃被征，而言事者多訟韙，復拜議郎，遷尚書。

translations.literal: Afterward when Chen Fan was summoned, many who spoke on affairs sued Wei; he was again appointed Gentleman Consultant and promoted Master of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Fan returned, critics attacked Diao Wei; he was reappointed Gentleman Consultant and rose to master of writing.

sentences.zh: 在朝有鯁直節，出為魯、東海二郡相。

translations.literal: At court he had the integrity of blunt uprightness; he went out as chancellor of Lu and Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: He was blunt at court, then served as chancellor of Lu and Donghai.

sentences.zh: 性抗厲，有明略，所在稱神。

translations.literal: By nature he was stern and keen, had clear strategy, and wherever he went was called spirit-like.
translations.idiomatic: Stern and shrewd, he was called uncanny in every post.

sentences.zh: 常以法度自整，家人莫見墯容焉。

translations.literal: He constantly regulated himself by law and statute; his household never saw a slackened expression.
translations.idiomatic: He kept strict discipline; his family never saw him relax.

sentences.zh: 琬被廢□幾二十年。

translations.literal: Wan was dismissed and [lacuna] nearly twenty years.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Wan lay proscribed almost twenty years.

sentences.zh: 至光和末，太尉楊賜上書薦琬有撥亂之才，由是征拜議郎，擢為青州刺史，遷侍中。

translations.literal: Down to the end of Guanghe, Grand Commandant Yang Ci submitted a memorial recommending Wan as having talent to set chaos in order; thereupon he was summoned and appointed Gentleman Consultant, was raised to Governor of Qingzhou, and was promoted palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: At Guanghe's end Yang Ci recommended his talent for saving a troubled age; he became Gentleman Consultant, governor of Qingzhou, then palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 中平初，出為右扶風，征拜將作大匠、少府、太僕。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Zhongping he went out as Governor of You Fufeng, was summoned and appointed Grand Master of Works, Minister of the Household, and Grand Coachman.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhongping's opening he governed You Fufeng, then served as grand master of works, minister of the household, and grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 又為豫州牧。

translations.literal: He again became Governor of Yu Province.
translations.idiomatic: He became governor of Yu Province.

sentences.zh: 時寇賊陸梁，州境彫殘，琬討擊平之，威聲大震。

translations.literal: At the time bandits swaggered; the province was wasted and ruined; Wan attacked and pacified them; his awe-inspiring name greatly shook.
translations.idiomatic: Bandits ravaged the province; he campaigned until order returned and his name terrified them.

sentences.zh: 政績為天下表，封關內侯。

translations.literal: His administrative achievements were a model for the realm under Heaven; he was enfeoffed marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: His record was the empire's model; he was enfeoffed marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 及董卓秉政，以琬名臣，征為司徒，遷太尉，更封陽泉鄉侯。

translations.literal: When Dong Zhuo held power, because Wan was a famous minister, he summoned him as Minister of Education, promoted him Grand Commandant, and changed his fief to village marquis of Yangquan.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo, needing a famous minister, made him minister of education, then grand commandant, with a new fief at Yangquan village.

sentences.zh: 卓議遷都長安，琬與司徒楊彪同諫不從。

translations.literal: When Zhuo debated moving the capital to Chang'an, Wan together with Minister of Education Yang Biao jointly remonstrated but was not followed.
translations.idiomatic: When Dong Zhuo proposed moving the capital to Chang'an, Huang Wan and Yang Biao remonstrated in vain.

sentences.zh: 琬退而駁議之曰：「昔周公營洛邑以寧姬，光武卜東都以隆漢，天之所啟，神之所安。

translations.literal: Wan withdrew and refuted the proposal, saying: "In antiquity the Duke of Zhou built Luoyi to secure the Ji; Guangwu divined the eastern capital to exalt the Han—what Heaven opens and the spirits settle.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Wan counter-memorialized: 'The Duke of Zhou built Luoyang to secure the Ji house; Guangwu chose the eastern capital to exalt Han—both followed Heaven and the spirits.'

sentences.zh: 大業既定，豈宜妄有遷動，以虧四海之望？」

translations.literal: The great enterprise already being settled, how can one rashly have removals and shifts, thereby to injure the expectation of the four seas?"
translations.idiomatic: The foundation is laid—how can you rashly move and forfeit the trust of the realm?'

sentences.zh: 時人懼卓暴怒，琬必及害，固諫之。

translations.literal: People of the time feared Zhuo's violent rage and that Wan would surely meet harm, and firmly remonstrated with him.
translations.idiomatic: Friends feared Dong Zhuo's temper and begged Huang Wan to yield.

sentences.zh: 琬對曰：「昔白公作亂於楚，屈廬冒刃而前；

translations.literal: Wan replied, saying: "In antiquity when the Duke of Bai made rebellion in Chu, Qu Lu braved the blade and stepped forward;
translations.idiomatic: He answered: 'When Duke Bai of Chu rebelled, Qu Lu stepped under naked blades;'

sentences.zh: [一]崔杼弒君於齊，晏嬰不懼其盟。

translations.literal: [1] when Cui Zhu assassinated his ruler in Qi, Yan Ying did not fear his covenant.
translations.idiomatic: [1] when Cui Zhu murdered his lord, Yan Ying defied his oath.'

sentences.zh: [二]吾雖不德，誠慕古人之節。

translations.literal: [2] Although I lack virtue, I truly admire the integrity of the men of antiquity."
translations.idiomatic: [2] I am no sage, yet I honor such courage.'

sentences.zh: 」琬竟坐免。

translations.literal: " Wan in the end sat and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: He was dismissed for it.

sentences.zh: 卓猶敬其名德舊族，不敢害。

translations.literal: Zhuo still respected his famous virtue and old lineage and did not dare harm him.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo still respected his name and clan and did not kill him.

sentences.zh: 後與楊彪同拜光祿大夫，及徙西都，轉司隸校尉，與司徒王允同謀誅卓。

translations.literal: Afterward together with Yang Biao he was appointed Grand Master of Splendid Carriages; when they moved to the western capital, he was transferred Metropolitan Superintendent and with Minister of Education Wang Yun jointly plotted to execute Zhuo.
translations.idiomatic: Later he and Yang Biao became grand masters of splendid carriages; at the move west he became metropolitan superintendent and joined Wang Yun's plot against Dong Zhuo.

sentences.zh: 及卓將李傕、郭汜攻破長安，遂收琬下獄死，時年五十二。

translations.literal: When Zhuo's generals Li Jue and Guo Si broke Chang'an, thereupon they seized Wan, put him in prison, and he died, aged fifty-two sui.
translations.idiomatic: Li Jue and Guo Si took Chang'an, seized Huang Wan, and killed him in prison at fifty-two.

sentences.zh: 注[一]新序曰：「白公勝*

translations.literal: Note [1] The Xin xu says: "When the Duke of Bai Sheng*
translations.idiomatic: Note one: the Xin xu says Duke Bai Sheng

translations.literal: (killed)
translations.idiomatic: (was about to kill)

sentences.zh: **[將弒]*楚惠王，王出亡，令尹、司馬皆死，勝拔□而屬之於屈廬曰：『子與我，將捨子，不我與，將殺子。

translations.literal: **[was about to assassinate]* King Hui of Chu, the king fled; the grand minister and minister of war both died; Sheng drew [lacuna] and pointed it at Qu Lu, saying: 'If you are with me, I will spare you; if you are not with me, I will kill you.
translations.idiomatic: plotted against King Hui of Chu; the king fled; high ministers died; Sheng threatened Qu Lu: 'Join me or die.'

sentences.zh: 』屈廬曰：『詩有之曰：「莫莫葛虆，延於條枚，愷悌君子，求福不回。

translations.literal: " Qu Lu said: 'The Poetry has it: "Luxuriant are the creepers, stretching along branches and twigs; joyous and easy is the nobleman, seeking blessing without turning back.
translations.idiomatic: Qu Lu quoted the Odes: 'Thick the vines along the boughs; the true gentleman seeks blessing without crooked paths.'

sentences.zh: 」今子殺子叔父而求福於廬也，可乎？

translations.literal: " Now you kill your uncle and seek blessing from Lu—can it be?
translations.idiomatic: You kill your kinsman yet ask blessing of me—can that work?'

sentences.zh: 且吾聞之，知命之士，見利不動，臨死則死，是謂人臣之禮。

translations.literal: Moreover I have heard that a man who knows fate, seeing profit does not move; facing death then dies—this is called the rite of a minister.
translations.idiomatic: A man who knows Heaven's mandate does not stir at gain and accepts death—that is a minister's duty.'

sentences.zh: 故上知天命，下知臣道。

translations.literal: Therefore above he knows Heaven's mandate, below he knows the way of a minister.
translations.idiomatic: Thus he knows Heaven above and service below.'

sentences.zh: 其有可劫乎？

translations.literal: Can such a one be coerced?
translations.idiomatic: Can you coerce such a man?'

sentences.zh: 子胡不推之！

translations.literal: Why do you not push this through!'
translations.idiomatic: Think again!'

sentences.zh: 』白公勝乃入其□焉。」

translations.literal: " The Duke of Bai Sheng thereupon entered his [lacuna]."
translations.idiomatic: Bai Gong then killed himself.

sentences.zh: 注[二]解見馮衍傳。

translations.literal: Note [2] The explanation is seen in the biography of Feng Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: see Feng Yan's biography.

sentences.zh: 論曰：古者諸侯歲貢士，進賢受上賞，非賢貶爵土。

translations.literal: The essay says: In antiquity the feudal lords yearly presented scholars; advancing the worthy received upper reward, presenting the unworthy demoted rank and territory.
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: feudal lords once presented talent yearly; recommending worthies won reward, sending dross cost rank.

sentences.zh: 升之司馬，辯論其才，論定然後官之，任官然後祿之。

translations.literal: They raised them to the Minister of War, debated their talents, fixed the evaluation and then gave them office, gave them office and then salary.
translations.idiomatic: Candidates went to the minister of war for debate, then office, then salary.

sentences.zh: [一]故王者得其人，進仕勸其行，經邦弘務，所由久矣。

translations.literal: [1] Therefore when the king obtained the right men, promotion encouraged their conduct; ordering the state and magnifying tasks—this is the source of long endurance.
translations.idiomatic: [1] Thus wise kings matched men to posts and built lasting rule.

sentences.zh: 漢初詔舉賢良、方正，州郡察孝廉、秀才，斯亦貢士之方也。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Han edicts summoned worthy, good, and upright men; provinces and commanderies inspected filial and incorrupt and cultivated talents—this too was the method of presenting scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Han edicts called for worthies; provinces nominated filial and cultivated talents—the same principle.

sentences.zh: 中興以後，復增敦樸、有道、賢能、直言、獨行、高節、質直、清白、敦厚之屬。

translations.literal: After the restoration, again were added honest and plain, possessed of the Way, worthy and able, straight-speaking, solitary in conduct, lofty in integrity, plain and straight, pure and white, honest and thick—and such categories.
translations.idiomatic: After Guangwu came further categories—honest, reclusive, outspoken, and the like.

sentences.zh: 榮路既廣，觖望難裁，自是竊名偽服，浸以流競。

translations.literal: With honor roads so wide, disappointed hopes were hard to restrain; from this stealing names and false garb gradually flowed into reckless competition.
translations.idiomatic: Broad paths to honor bred false reputations and fierce rivalry.

sentences.zh: 權門貴仕，請謁繁興。

translations.literal: Powerful gates and noble offices—seeking favor and visiting flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Great houses and high posts traded favors openly.

sentences.zh: 自左雄任事，限年試才，雖頗有不密，固亦因識時宜。

translations.literal: From the time Zuo Xiong held office, limiting years and testing talent—although there were places not tight, it solidly was because he recognized the times' needs.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Xiong's age limits and exams, though imperfect, fit the age.

sentences.zh: 而黃瓊、胡廣、張衡、崔瑗之徒，泥滯舊方，互相詭駁，循名者屈其短，筭實者挺其□。

translations.literal: Yet Huang Qiong, Hu Guang, Zhang Heng, Cui Yuan, and such men, stuck in old patterns, mutually made strange refutations; those who followed names bent their shortcomings, those who calculated substance straightened their [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong, Hu Guang, Zhang Heng, and Cui Yuan clung to old rules and crossed purposes—formalists hid faults, realists exposed them.

sentences.zh: 故雄在尚書，天下不敢妄選，十餘年閒，稱為得人，斯亦□實之征乎？

translations.literal: Therefore while Xiong was at the Masters of Writing, the realm under Heaven did not dare recklessly select; within more than ten years he was called to have obtained the right men—is this not a sign of [lacuna] substance?
translations.idiomatic: Under Zuo Xiong the capital dared not traffic in office for a decade—proof that substance could be tested.

sentences.zh: 順帝始以童弱反政，而號令自出，知能任使，故士得用情，天下喁喁仰其風采。

translations.literal: When Emperor Shun first took the government back from childhood, yet commands issued from himself, knowing how to employ men, therefore scholars could use their sincerity; all under Heaven craned their necks admiring his bearing.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Shun, though young, held real power, chose men well, and the empire looked up to him.

sentences.zh: 遂乃備玄纁玉帛，以聘南陽樊英，天子降寢殿，設□席，尚書奉引，延問失得。

translations.literal: Thereupon he prepared dark and light silks and jade and silk to hire Fan Ying of Nanyang; the Son of Heaven descended to the sleeping hall, spread [lacuna] mats, the Master of Writing led the way, and extended inquiry on gains and losses.
translations.idiomatic: He sent black and yellow silks to hire Fan Ying, received him below the imperial couch, and asked what was wrong with the state.

sentences.zh: 急登賢之舉，虛降己之禮，於是處士鄙生，忘其拘儒，[二]拂巾衽褐，以企旌車之招矣。

translations.literal: Urgent in elevating the worthy, humble in lowering himself—in this way recluses and rustic scholars forgot their cramped Confucianism, [2] brushed caps and wore coarse cloth, hoping for the summoning carriage's invitation.
translations.idiomatic: Such urgency to find talent and humility toward scholars made hermits dust off caps and wait for the imperial carriage.

sentences.zh: 至乃英能承風，俊乂鹹事，若李固、周舉之淵謨弘深，左雄、黃瓊之政事貞固，桓焉、楊厚以儒學進，崔瑗、馬融以文章顯，吳佑、蘇章、種暠、欒巴牧民之良干，龐參、虞詡將帥之宏規，王龔、張皓虛心以推士，張綱、杜喬直道以糾違，郎顗陰陽詳密，張衡機術特妙：東京之士，於茲盛焉。

translations.literal: Thus even Ying could catch the wind, and the talented all served—such as the deep and broad counsel of Li Gu and Zhou Ju, the upright administration of Zuo Xiong and Huang Qiong, Huan Yan and Yang Hou advancing by Confucian learning, Cui Yuan and Ma Rong displaying literary brilliance, Wu You, Su Zhang, Chong Hao, and Luan Ba as fine tools for shepherding the people, Pang Shen and Yu Xu as grand models for generals, Wang Gong and Zhang Hao with open mind recommending scholars, Zhang Gang and Du Qiao with straight path correcting violations, Lang Yi detailed in yin-yang, Zhang Heng uniquely subtle in devices and arts: the scholars of eastern capital flourished here.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Ying answered the call; Li Gu, Zhou Ju, Zuo Xiong, Huang Qiong, Huan Yan, Yang Hou, Cui Yuan, Ma Rong, Wu You, Su Zhang, Chong Hao, Luan Ba, Pang Shen, Yu Xu, Wang Gong, Zhang Hao, Zhang Gang, Du Qiao, Lang Yi, and Zhang Heng—statesmen, generals, and scientists—made the eastern capital bloom.

sentences.zh: 向使廟堂納其高謀，強*

translations.literal: If the court had accepted their high plans, and on the hard-fought*
translations.idiomatic: Had high policy taken their counsel, and on every contested

translations.literal: (battle)
translations.idiomatic: (battle)

sentences.zh: **[埸]*宣其智力，帷幄容其謇辭，舉厝稟其成式，則武、宣之軌，豈其遠而？

translations.literal: **[frontier]* field commanders had deployed their wit and strength, if the inner tent had welcomed blunt counsel and every decision had followed their proven models—would the age of Wu and Xuan be far away?
translations.idiomatic: frontier, generals had matched wit to force, while the inner court heard blunt truth and the throne followed their models—the glory of Emperors Wu and Xuan would lie within reach.

sentences.zh: [三]詩云：「靡不有初，鮮克有終。

translations.literal: [3] The Poetry says: "There is none without a beginning; few can keep the end.
translations.idiomatic: [3] The Odes say, 'Few can finish what they begin.'

sentences.zh: 」可為恨哉！

translations.literal: " It can be counted a regret!
translations.idiomatic: A lasting regret!'

sentences.zh: 及孝桓之時，碩德繼興，[四]陳蕃、楊秉處稱賢宰，皇甫、張、段出號名將，王暢、李膺彌縫袞闕，[五]朱穆、劉陶獻替匡時，郭有道□鑒人倫，陳仲弓弘道下邑。

translations.literal: By the time of Emperor Huan, great virtue arose in succession, [4] Chen Fan and Yang Bing at court were called worthy chancellors, Huangfu, Zhang, and Duan abroad were famed generals, Wang Chang and Li Ying patched the emperor's robe, [5] Zhu Mu and Liu Tao offered counsel to correct the age, Guo Tai [lacuna] mirrored human relations, Chen Zhong expanded the Way in a lowly county.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Huan came Chen Fan, Yang Bing, Huangfu Song, Zhang Huan, Duan Jiong, Wang Chang, Li Ying, Zhu Mu, Liu Tao, Guo Tai, and Chen Shi—statesmen who shored up the throne.

sentences.zh: 其餘宏儒遠智，高心絜行，激揚風流者，不可勝言。

translations.literal: The rest—great Confucians and far-seeing men, high in mind and pure in conduct, who stirred the currents—cannot all be told.
translations.idiomatic: Other great scholars and pure men are too many to list.

sentences.zh: 而斯道莫振，文武陵隊，在朝者以正議嬰戮，謝事者以黨錮致□。

translations.literal: Yet this way did not flourish; civil and military fell in ranks; those at court met slaughter for upright counsel; those who retired met proscription from faction.
translations.idiomatic: Yet the way withered: courtiers died for speaking truth; retirees were caught in faction bans.

sentences.zh: 往車雖折，而來軫方遒。

translations.literal: The forward carriage though broken, yet the coming axle-boxes are still strong.
translations.idiomatic: The front axle snapped, but the rear wheels still thundered.

sentences.zh: [六] 所以傾而未顛，決而未潰，豈非仁人君子心力之為乎？

translations.literal: [6] That therefore it leaned yet did not topple, burst yet did not collapse—is it not the work of the hearts and strength of humane gentlemen?
translations.idiomatic: [6] The dynasty leaned yet stood, cracked yet held—thanks to humane men who braced it.

sentences.zh: 嗚呼！

translations.literal: Ah—!
translations.idiomatic: What a pity it is to say so.

sentences.zh: 注[一]尚書大傳曰「古者諸侯之於天子，三年一貢士。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Great Tradition on the Documents says: "In antiquity the feudal lords toward the Son of Heaven, once in three years presented one scholar."
translations.idiomatic: Note one: the Shangshu dazhuan says regional lords presented one candidate every three years.

sentences.zh: 一適謂之好德，再適謂之賢賢，三適謂之有功。

translations.literal: One meeting was called 'loving virtue,' a second meeting 'honoring the worthy,' a third meeting 'having merit.'
translations.idiomatic: The first acceptable tribute was 'loves virtue,' the second 'honors worthies,' the third 'has merit.'

sentences.zh: 有功者，天子賜以車服弓矢，號曰命。

translations.literal: Those with merit—the Son of Heaven bestowed chariot robes, bows and arrows, and called it a mandate.
translations.idiomatic: Meritorious lords received chariot dress, bow and arrows, and a formal mandate.

sentences.zh: 諸侯有不貢士謂之不率正，一不適謂之過，再不適謂之傲，三不適謂之誣。

translations.literal: Feudal lords who did not present scholars were called 'not leading correctly'; one failure was called 'fault,' a second 'arrogance,' a third 'deceit.'
translations.idiomatic: Lords who sent no talent were 'not rectifying'; one miss was a fault, two arrogance, three deceit.

sentences.zh: 誣者，天子絀之，一絀以爵，再絀以地，三絀而爵地畢」也。

translations.literal: The deceitful—the Son of Heaven degraded them: first degradation by rank, second by land, third and rank and land were exhausted."
translations.idiomatic: After three failures the throne stripped rank, then land, then both.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]拘儒猶褊狹也。

translations.literal: Note [2] 'Cramped Confucian' means narrow and cramped.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: 'petty pedant' means narrow-minded.

sentences.zh: 注[三]而，語辭也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Er is a particle.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: er is a filler particle.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「豈不獸思，室是遠而。」

translations.literal: The Analects says: "How could I not think of you? Your chamber is far away."
translations.idiomatic: The Analects line: 'I long for you, though your home is far.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]碩，大也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Shuo means great.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: shuo means great.

sentences.zh: 注[五]彌縫猶補合也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Mi feng means to patch and join.
translations.idiomatic: Note five: mi feng means to darn or patch.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「袞職有闕，惟仲山甫補之。」

translations.literal: The Poetry says: "Where the emperor's robe had a gap, only Zhong Shanfu mended it."
translations.idiomatic: The Odes: 'Where the royal robe tore, Zhong Shanfu patched it.'

sentences.zh: 注[六]廣雅曰：「遒，急也。」

translations.literal: Note [6] The Guangya says: "Qiu means urgent."
translations.idiomatic: Note six: Guangya glosses qiu as urgent.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：雄作納言，古之八元。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Xiong held the intake of words—one of the ancient Eight Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Encomium: Zuo Xiong spoke for the throne among the legendary Eight Yuan ministers.

sentences.zh: 舉升以匯，越自下蕃。

translations.literal: Ju rose from the throng, crossing up from a lowly border.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Ju rose from the margins into the center.

sentences.zh: [一]登朝理政，並紓□昏。

translations.literal: [1] They ascended court and ordered government, together loosening the dimmed age.
translations.idiomatic: [1] At court they straightened policy and eased a dark time.'

sentences.zh: [二]瓊名夙知，累章國疵。

translations.literal: [2] Qiong's name long known, he piled up memorials on the state's flaws.
translations.idiomatic: [2] Huang Qiong's fame was old; he exposed the realm's sickness in memorial after memorial.'

sentences.zh: [三]琬亦早秀，位及志差。

translations.literal: [3] Wan too flowered early; he reached high rank but his aims went awry.
translations.idiomatic: [3] Huang Wan bloomed young yet high office and true will parted ways.'

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]匯，類也。

translations.literal: [4] Note [1] Hui means category.
translations.idiomatic: [4] Note one: hui means kind or class.'

sentences.zh: 易曰：「以其匯征吉。

translations.literal: The Changes says: "By means of his kind, the expedition is auspicious."
translations.idiomatic: The Yi: 'March with your proper company and it is auspicious.'

sentences.zh: 」匯音謂。

translations.literal: " Hui is read wei.
translations.idiomatic: The character hui is read wei.

translations.literal: Note [2] Shu means to loosen; it is read shi yu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: shu means to untie; read shi yu fan.

sentences.zh: 注[三]疵，病也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Ci means ailment.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: ci means fault or illness.

sentences.zh: 注[四]志意差舛，不能遂也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Aim and intent went awry and could not be fulfilled.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: his purpose went astray and was never fulfilled.

sentences.zh: 差音楚宜反。

translations.literal: Chai is read chu yi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Chai is read chu yi fan.

translations.idiomatic: Page 2015 line 3: Nan

translations.idiomatic: (commandery)

sentences.zh: **[陽]*□陽人也集解引洪亮吉說，謂「郡」應作「陽」，刊寫之誤。

translations.literal: The gloss on Yang of Nieyang: the Jijie cites Hong Liangji that the character written as commandery should read Yang, a carving error.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Liangji: 'commandery' is a misprint for Yang in the place name.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The text is corrected accordingly.

sentences.zh: 二０一五頁一二行臣聞柔遠和邇按：校補引柳從辰說，謂閩本「聞」下有「之」字。

translations.literal: Page 2015 line 12: 'Your subject has heard soften the distant'—Collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: the Min edition has the particle zhi after wen.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen notes the Min edition inserts zhi after 'heard.'

sentences.zh: 二０一六頁二行興雨祁祁按：王先謙謂據注「興雨」當作「興雲」。

translations.literal: Page 2016 line 2: xing yu qi qi—Wang Xianqian argues from the commentary that xing yu should be xing yun.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xianqian: the line should read xing yun (rising clouds), not xing yu.

sentences.zh: 此用三家詩，而後人據毛改之。

translations.literal: This uses the three-house version of the Poetry; later editors altered it following the Mao text.
translations.idiomatic: The line follows the three-house Odes; a later hand changed it to match Mao.

sentences.zh: 二０一六頁一五行□艷謂□姒也艷色美也集解引錢大昕說，謂章懷注用毛氏說，鄭康成則以艷妻為厲王后，謂正月惡□姒滅周，十月之交疾艷妻煽方處，則「□艷」非一人。

translations.literal: Page 2016 line 15 on Baoyan: Qian Daxin observes Zhang Hua's commentary follows Mao while Zheng Xuan treats the consort called yan as King Li's queen and argues the figure is not the same person in every passage.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin: Zhang Hua follows Mao on Baoyan; Zheng Xuan identifies her with King Li's consort—so Baoyan is not one person across all traditions.

sentences.zh: 此疏上言「幽、厲昏亂」，下言「□艷用權」，則亦與鄭說同。

translations.literal: This memorial above says 'You and Li were benighted'; below says 'Baoyan exercised power'—thus it agrees with Zheng's reading.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial pairs You and Li with Baoyan's power—aligning with Zheng Xuan.

sentences.zh: 魯詩「艷」作「閻」，尚書中候作「剡」。

translations.literal: The Lu version writes yan as yan (homophone graph); the Shangshu zhonghou writes it jian.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Odes variant yan; Shangshu zhonghou writes jian.

sentences.zh: 閻、剡、艷文異實同，蓋其女族姓，非訓美色也。

translations.literal: Yan, jian, and yan differ in script but match the same clan surname; it is not glossed as 'beautiful color.'
translations.idiomatic: The graphs differ but denote the same clan, not 'beauty.'

sentences.zh: 二０一七頁一行厲王淫於色殿本「厲」作「幽」。

translations.literal: Page 2017 line 1: 'King Li was dissolute in sex'—the palace edition writes You for Li.
translations.idiomatic: Palace edition reads King You instead of King Li.

sentences.zh: 按：用毛說當作「幽」，依鄭說應作「厲」也。

translations.literal: Collation: following Mao one should write You; following Zheng one should write Li.
translations.idiomatic: Mao tradition favors You; Zheng favors Li.

sentences.zh: 二０一八頁八行問畜貨聚*

translations.literal: Page 2018 line 8: 'asking about hoarding goods and gathering*'
translations.idiomatic: Line 8: hoarding goods and gathering

translations.literal: (there)
translations.idiomatic: (there)

sentences.zh: **[馬]*刊誤謂案國語作「聚馬」，此誤。

translations.literal: Kanwu on the gloss for horses: the Guoyu text reads gather horses; the received wording here is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Guoyu reads 'horses'; emend accordingly.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Corrected.

sentences.zh: 二０一九頁一五行詔試明經者補弟子按：順帝紀「明經」下有「下第」二字。

translations.literal: Page 2019 line 15: edict testing classicists to fill disciple places—Shun's annals add 'failed the lower rank' after mingjing.
translations.idiomatic: Shun's annals insert xia di after mingjing.

sentences.zh: 二０二０頁一行諸王國郎者百三十八人按：張□謂「者」字衍。

translations.literal: Page 2020 line 1: 'one hundred thirty-eight men as Gentlemen to princely kingdoms'—Zhang says the particle zhe is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang: delete redundant zhe.

sentences.zh: 二０二０頁一０行迄於永*

translations.literal: Page 2020 line 10: down to Yong*
translations.idiomatic: Page 2020 line 10: Yong

translations.literal: (jia)
translations.idiomatic: (jia)

sentences.zh: **[□]*「永嘉」乃「永□」之鬥，今改，詳珍帝紀校勘記。

translations.literal: The characters read as Yongjia are a miswriting of the correct Yong-era name; they are now corrected; see the collation in the annals of Emperor Zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Yongjia misprinted; see Zhi's collation.

sentences.zh: 汲本、殿本作「永熹」，錢大昭謂「熹」乃「□」之鬥。

translations.literal: Ji and palace editions read Yongxi; Qian Dazhao says xi is a misprint for the lacuna character.
translations.idiomatic: Yongxi is another misprint.

sentences.zh: 二０二０頁一一行故稱家*[法]*據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2020 line 11: therefore called house learning—supplied from Ji and palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: The text restores the phrase jia fa from the Ji and Dian editions.

sentences.zh: 二０二０頁一二行淑字伯進按：殿本「伯進」作「伯達」。

translations.literal: Page 2020 line 12: Shu courtesy Bojin—palace edition writes Boda for Bojin.
translations.idiomatic: Palace edition: Boda for Bojin.

sentences.zh: 二０二二頁七行九卿位亞三事按：集解引惠棟說，謂東觀記「三事」作「三公」。

translations.literal: Page 2022 line 7: nine ministers rank next to the three affairs—Jijie cites Hui Dong: Dongguan ji writes three dukes for three affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong: Dongguan ji has three gong.

sentences.zh: 二０二三頁二行周舉字宣光校補引柳從辰說，謂書鈔七十二引續漢書作「字真先」。

translations.literal: Page 2023 line 2: Zhou Ju courtesy Xuanguang—Collation supplement cites Liu: Shuchao 72 cites Xu Han shu as 'courtesy Zhenxian.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen: variant courtesy Zhenxian in Shuchao.

sentences.zh: 按：類聚五十、御覽二百五十六引無「字真先」三字。

translations.literal: Collation: Leiju 50 and Yulan 256 omit the three characters for the variant courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: Leiju and Yulan omit the variant.

sentences.zh: 二０二三頁四行延*

translations.literal: Page 2023 line 4: Yan*
translations.idiomatic: Yan

translations.literal: (xi)
translations.idiomatic: (xi)

sentences.zh: **[光]*四年據集解引錢大昕說改。

translations.literal: The fourth year of the Yanguang era is emended per the Jijie citation of Qian Daxin.
translations.idiomatic: Yanguang 4 per Qian Daxin.

sentences.zh: 二０二三頁五行謀殺嚴公嚴公誓之黃泉汲本、殿本「嚴」並作「莊」。

translations.literal: Page 2023 line 5: plot to kill Yan Duke—Yan Duke swore Yellow Springs—Ji and palace write Zhuang for Yan (taboo avoidance).
translations.idiomatic: Yan/Zhuang taboo: editions vary.

sentences.zh: 按：此避明帝諱，未回改也。

translations.literal: Collation: taboo for Ming emperor; not restored in this print.
translations.idiomatic: Ming taboo not reversed here.

sentences.zh: 二０二四頁二行今詔怒按：集解引何焯說，謂「怒」下疑有脫文。

translations.literal: Page 2024 line 2: 'now the edict is angry'—Jijie cites He Zhuo: suspect lacuna after nu.
translations.idiomatic: He Zhuo: possible lacuna after 'angry.'

sentences.zh: 二０二四頁三行位為台輔按：汲本、殿本「為」作「至」。

translations.literal: Page 2024 line 3: 'position as terrace pillar'—Ji edition writes zhi for wei.
translations.idiomatic: Ji edition: zhi for wei.

sentences.zh: 二０二四頁八行尚書郭虔按：集解引汪文台說，謂御覽五九四引張璠漢記，謂「尚書郭度見之歎息，上疏願退位避舉」。

translations.literal: Page 2024 line 8 on Master of Writing Guo Qian: the Jijie cites Wang Wentai that Yulan 594's Zhang Fan Han ji records the name as Guo Du and has him sigh and offer to yield his post to avoid the nomination.
translations.idiomatic: Variant name Guo Du in Zhang Fan.

sentences.zh: 「虔」作「度」，未知孰是。

translations.literal: Qian versus Du—which is correct is uncertain.
translations.idiomatic: Qian or Du uncertain.

sentences.zh: 二０二五頁一０行順四節之宜按：汲本、殿本「節」作「時」。

translations.literal: Page 2025 line 10: 'comply with the four seasons'—Ji and palace write shi for jie.
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Dian: shi for jie.

sentences.zh: 二０二六頁四行猶緣木希魚□行求前汲本、殿本「希魚」作「求魚」。

translations.literal: Page 2026 line 4 on climbing a tree for fish and walking backward to advance: Ji and palace editions write qiu yu for xi yu.
translations.idiomatic: Editions read qiu yu for xi yu.

sentences.zh: 按：腢書治要亦作「希魚」。

translations.literal: Collation: Qunshu zhiyao also has xi yu.
translations.idiomatic: Zhiyao agrees with xi yu.

sentences.zh: 李慈銘謂此因下文有「求」字而避易，今本乃據孟子妄改之。

translations.literal: Li Ciming says this avoids repeating qiu from the next line; modern text wrongly changes it from Mencius.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ciming: xi avoids repetition; later editors wrongly aligned with Mencius.

sentences.zh: 二０二六頁一二行事見白武通汲本、殿本「武」作「虎」。

translations.literal: Page 2026 line 12: 'affair seen in Baiwu tong'—Ji and palace write hu for wu (Tang taboo).
translations.idiomatic: Tang taboo: hu for wu in Baihu tong.

sentences.zh: 按：此避唐諱，未回改也。

translations.literal: Collation: Tang taboo not reversed in this print.
translations.idiomatic: Tang taboo unreversed.

sentences.zh: 二０二七頁四行解見楊厚傳按：集解引惠棟說，謂楊厚傳無此注，黃瓊傳有之。

translations.literal: Page 2027 line 4: 'explanation seen in Yang Hou's biography'—Jijie cites Hui Dong: Yang Hou's biography lacks this note; it belongs to Huang Qiong's.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong: gloss misplaced from Huang Qiong.

sentences.zh: 二０二八頁八行燕於洛水按：「於」原作「乎」，逕據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: Page 2028 line 8: 'sacrificed on the Luo'—original hu emended to yu per Ji and palace.
translations.idiomatic: hu to yu emendation.

sentences.zh: 二０二八頁一四行□上露何易晞按：集解引李良裘說，謂按古今注「露」上有「朝」字，以七字為句。

translations.literal: Page 2028 line 14: dew on top how easily dry—Jijie cites Li: Gujin zhu should have chao before lu for a seven-character line.
translations.idiomatic: Li: insert chao before lu per Gujin zhu.

sentences.zh: 二０二九頁八行兗州刺史郭遵集解引汪文台說，謂御覽七七八引續漢書，「郭遵」作「甄遵」。

translations.literal: Page 2029 line 8: Governor of Yan Guo Zun—Jijie cites Wang: Yulan 778 cites Xu Han shu as Zhen Zun.
translations.idiomatic: Wang: variant Zhen Zun.

sentences.zh: 二０三０頁一五行尹翁歸為右扶風*[卒]*據刊誤補。

translations.literal: Page 2030 line 15 on Yin Wengui as governor of You Fufeng: the Kanwu collation supplies the graph meaning died.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu supplies died.

translations.literal: (Poetry)
translations.idiomatic: (Poetry)

translations.idiomatic: Stray duplicate line removed per editions.

sentences.zh: 二０三一頁七行致敬於勰按：「敬」原斗「教」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2031 line 7: 'paid respect to Xie'—original jiao misprinted as jiao for jing; emended per Ji and palace.
translations.idiomatic: jiao to jing emendation.

sentences.zh: 二０三二頁八行常聞語曰汲本「常」作「嘗」。

translations.literal: Page 2032 line 8: 'often heard the saying'—Ji edition writes chang for chang (variant).
translations.idiomatic: chang/chang variant.

sentences.zh: 按：嘗常通。

translations.literal: Collation: chang and chang interchange.
translations.idiomatic: The two chang graphs interchange.

sentences.zh: 二０三四頁六行公大驚懼按：「公」原斗「人」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2034 line 6: 'the duke greatly feared'—original ren misprinted for gong; emended per Ji and palace.
translations.idiomatic: ren to gong emendation.

translations.literal: Page 2034 line 6: Guo Du*
translations.idiomatic: Guo Du

translations.idiomatic: (zhi)

translations.idiomatic: Kanwu deletion.

sentences.zh: 二０三五頁三行順時覛土按：「覛」原斗「□」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 2035 line 3: 'observe the seasons and inspect the soil'—original lacuna emended to mi per editions.
translations.idiomatic: mi restored for lacuna.

sentences.zh: 又按：「覛」字見說文□部，汲本、殿本作「□」，亦誤。

translations.literal: Also: mi appears in Shuowen; Ji and palace misprint another graph.
translations.idiomatic: Further note on mi variant.

sentences.zh: 二０三五頁一五行以宜比周公刊誤謂「以宜」當作「宜以」。

translations.literal: Page 2035 line 15: yi yi bi Zhou gong—Kanwu says word order should be yi yi Zhou gong.
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu: transpose yi yi.

sentences.zh: 集解引沉欽韓說，謂袁紀無「以」字，更順。

translations.literal: Jijie cites Shen Qinhan: Yuan ji lacks yi—smoother.
translations.idiomatic: Shen: Yuan ji omits yi.

sentences.zh: 按：原本「以」字漫漶，逕據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Collation: original yi faded; supplied from Ji and palace.
translations.idiomatic: Faded yi restored from editions.

sentences.zh: 二０三六頁一行增邑三千按：「三千」原作「三十」，然查張元濟校勘記，謂「十」字板損宜修，則原本「十」字或亦作「千」也。

translations.literal: Page 2036 line 1: increase fief three thousand—original thirty emended to three thousand per Zhang Yuanji's collation.
translations.idiomatic: The collation note explains that the figure thirty was misprinted and should read three thousand households, following Zhang Yuanji's verification of the woodblock damage.

sentences.zh: 今從汲本、殿本。

translations.literal: Now following Ji and palace.
translations.idiomatic: Follow Ji and Dian.

sentences.zh: 二０三六頁六行啟獸土宇按：今詩作「大啟爾宇」。

translations.literal: Page 2036 line 6: qi shou tu yu—modern Poetry reads da qi er yu.
translations.idiomatic: Variant from Mao text.

sentences.zh: 二０三六頁一一行為冀所飾舉者按：汲本「飾」作「辟」。

translations.literal: Page 2036 line 11: 'those whom Ji recommended'—Ji edition writes pi for shi.
translations.idiomatic: Ji: pi for shi.

sentences.zh: 二０三六頁一二行司徒韓演按：惠棟補注謂風俗通「演」作「演」。

translations.literal: Page 2036 line 12: Minister Han Yan—Hui Dong: Fengsu tong writes the name differently.
translations.idiomatic: Fengsu tong name variant.

sentences.zh: 二０三七頁六行則以賢者為力袁宏紀作「則以忠賢為助」。

translations.literal: Page 2037 line 6: 'then take the worthy as strength'—Yuan Hong ji reads 'then take loyal worthies as help.'
translations.idiomatic: Yuan ji variant with zhong xian.

sentences.zh: 按：「忠賢」與上「德義」相對成文，當從袁紀。

translations.literal: Collation: zhong xian pairs with de yi above; follow Yuan ji.
translations.idiomatic: Prefer Yuan ji wording.

sentences.zh: 二０三八頁一行念國亡身殿本「亡」作「忘」。

translations.literal: Page 2038 line 1: 'thought on the state and forgot his person'—palace edition writes wang for wu.
translations.idiomatic: Palace: wang for wu variant.

sentences.zh: 按：亡忘通。

translations.literal: Collation: wu and wang interchange.
translations.idiomatic: wu/wang interchange.

sentences.zh: 二０三八頁一一行*[阿黨]*相抑集解引王補說，謂袁紀作「阿黨相抑」。

translations.literal: Page 2038 line 11: *[faction]* mutually suppress—Jijie cites Wang: Yuan ji has 'faction mutually suppress.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang: Yuan ji has a dang.

sentences.zh: 按：「阿黨相抑」與上「讒諛所舉」相對成文，今依袁紀補「阿黨」二字。

translations.literal: Collation: a dang pairs with slander above; now supplied per Yuan ji.
translations.idiomatic: a dang restored per Yuan ji.

sentences.zh: 二０三八頁一二行敢以垂絕之日袁紀作「敢以垂死之年」。

translations.literal: Page 2038 line 12: 'dare on the day of hanging thread'—Yuan ji reads 'dare in the years of nearing death.'
translations.idiomatic: Yuan ji: chui si nian for chui jue zhi ri.

sentences.zh: 按：袁紀瓊上疏在延熹二年，雲會單超等五侯□權，瓊自度力不能制，乃稱疾不朝，上表曰云云，與此雲七年疾篤上疏諫異，措辭亦不同也。

translations.literal: Collation: the Yuan ji places Huang Qiong's memorial in Yanxi 2, saying that when Shan Chao and the five marquises monopolized power, Qiong measured himself as unable to control them, claimed illness and did not attend court, and submitted a memorial beginning thus and so, which differs from this passage saying that in the seventh year, gravely ill, he remonstrated—the wording is also not the same.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuan ji dates Huang Qiong's protest to Yanxi 2, when the five marquises seized power and he feigned illness rather than face court; that text differs from this chapter's account of a deathbed memorial in the seventh year.

sentences.zh: 二０三八頁一三行謚曰忠侯按：惠棟補注謂袁紀作「昭侯」。

translations.literal: Page 2038 line 13: posthumous title Loyal Marquis—Hui Dong's supplementary note says the Yuan ji writes Bright Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong notes the Yuan ji gives his posthumous title as Zhao (Bright) rather than Zhong (Loyal).

sentences.zh: 二０三九頁七行則蛟龍不合陰陽汲本、殿本「不合陰陽」作「不處其淵」。

translations.literal: Page 2039 line 7: 'then the horned dragon does not join yin and yang'—Ji and palace editions write 'does not dwell in its deep pool' instead of 'does not join yin and yang.'
translations.idiomatic: Ji and Dian editions read 'the dragon will not stay in its pool' instead of 'will not harmonize yin and yang.'

sentences.zh: 按：史記孔子世家作「不合陰陽」，今本家語困誓篇作「不處其淵」。

translations.literal: Collation: the Shiji, 'Confucius' hereditary house,' has 'does not join yin and yang'; the received Jia yu, 'Kun shi' chapter, has 'does not dwell in its deep pool.'
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji agrees with 'yin and yang'; the current Jia yu reads 'deep pool.'

sentences.zh: 二０三九頁一五行琬字子琰按：集解引惠棟說，謂文選注引范書作「公琰」。

translations.literal: Page 2039 line 15 on Wan courtesy Ziyan—Jijie cites Hui Dong: the Wenxuan commentary's citation of Fan Ye's History writes Gongyan.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong: the Wenxuan commentary quotes his courtesy as Gongyan instead of Ziyan.

sentences.zh: 二０三九頁一五行少失父按：集解引惠棟說，謂文選注引云「少失父母」。

translations.literal: Page 2039 line 15 on 'in youth he lost his father'—Jijie cites Hui Dong: the Wenxuan commentary's citation reads 'in youth he lost father and mother.'
translations.idiomatic: The Wenxuan citation adds that he lost both parents in childhood.

sentences.zh: 二０三九頁一五行祖父瓊初為魏郡太守按：集解引惠棟說，謂文選注引云「祖父瓊育之，初為魏郡太守」云云也。

translations.literal: Page 2039 line 15 on 'his grandfather Qiong, when he first was Grand Administrator of Wei'—Jijie cites Hui Dong: the Wenxuan commentary's citation reads 'his grandfather Qiong raised him; when he first was Grand Administrator of Wei,' and so on.
translations.idiomatic: The Wenxuan citation says Huang Qiong raised him before serving as Wei governor.

sentences.zh: 二０四０頁一０行事下御史*[中]*丞王暢據汲本補。

translations.literal: Page 2040 line 10: 'the affair went down to Imperial Clerk [Central] Director Wang Chang'—the graph zhong supplied per Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: The received text lacked 'central' in censor-in-chief; the Ji edition restores it.

sentences.zh: 二０四一頁一二行白公勝*

translations.literal: Page 2041 line 12: the Duke of Bai Sheng*
translations.idiomatic: Line 12: Bai Gong Sheng

translations.literal: (assassinate)
translations.idiomatic: (assassinate)

sentences.zh: **[將弒]*楚惠王據今新序增刪。

translations.literal: was about to assassinate King Hui of Chu—emended per the modern Xin xu additions and deletions.
translations.idiomatic: The line on plotting against King Hui follows the revised Xin xu.

sentences.zh: 二０四一頁一四行見利不動臨死則死是謂人臣之禮按：校補引柳從辰說，謂今新序作「見利不動，臨死不恐，為人臣者，時生則生，時死則死」。

translations.literal: Page 2041 line 14: 'seeing profit he does not move; facing death then dies—this is called the rite of a minister'—Collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: the modern Xin xu reads 'seeing profit he does not move; facing death he is not afraid; as a minister of men, when it is time to live he lives, when it is time to die he dies.'
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen: the Xin xu expands the minister's duty with lines on fearlessness and timely life or death.

sentences.zh: 二０四二頁四行賢能按：「賢能」上原衍「仁」字，逕據汲本、殿本刪。

translations.literal: Page 2042 line 4: worthy and able—Collation: the graph ren was spuriously prefixed above 'worthy and able' and is deleted straightway per Ji and palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: A stray ren before xian neng is removed per the standard editions.

sentences.zh: 二０四二頁六行固亦因識時宜按：刊誤謂案文當作「因時識宜」。

translations.literal: Page 2042 line 6: 'solidly was because he recognized the times' needs—Kanwu says examining the text it should read 'because of the times he recognized what was fitting.'
translations.idiomatic: Kanwu transposes the phrase to yi shi shi yi (know the time and the fit).

sentences.zh: 二０四二頁一四行強*

translations.literal: Page 2042 line 14: qiang*
translations.idiomatic: Line 14: qiang (strong)

translations.literal: (battlefield)
translations.idiomatic: (battlefield)

sentences.zh: **[埸]*宣其智力據汲本改。

translations.literal: field commanders should display their wit and strength—emended per Ji edition.
translations.idiomatic: The compound for battlefield command is restored from the Ji edition.
